Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىِ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١)
O you who have faith! Do not venture ahead of Allah and His Apostle, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (1)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَرْفَعُوٓا۟ أَصْوَٰتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ ٱلنَّبِىِّ وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا۟ لَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ أَن تَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ (٢)
O you who have faith! Do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet, and do not speak aloud to him like you shout to one another, lest your works should fail without your being aware. (2)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَغُضُّونَ أَصْوَٰتَهُمْ عِندَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱمْتَحَنَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٣)
Indeed those who lower their voices in the presence of the Apostle of Allah—they are the ones whose hearts Allah has tested for Godwariness. For them will be forgiveness and a great reward. (3)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنَادُونَكَ مِن وَرَآءِ ٱلْحُجُرَٰتِ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٤)
Indeed those who call you from behind the apartments, most of them do not use their reason. (4)
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ صَبَرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ تَخْرُجَ إِلَيْهِمْ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥)
Had they been patient until you came out for them, it would have been better for them, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)

Content of Surah al-Hujurat

In this Surah, which consists of no more than eighteen verses, very important issues regarding the person of the Prophet and the relationship of the Islamic society with one another are raised. Since many significant ethical matters are addressed within it, it can be called the "Surah of Ethics and Manners."

Overall, the various sections of this Surah can be summarized as follows:

The first section: These are the opening verses of the Surah, which explain the etiquette of interacting with the great leader of Islam, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the principles that Muslims must apply in His presence.

The second section: This Surah contains a series of important principles of "social ethics." Implementing them preserves love, purity, sincerity, security, and unity within the Islamic society; conversely, forgetting them leads to pessimism, hypocrisy, division, and insecurity.

The third section: These are instructions related to how to combat disputes and conflicts that may occasionally occur among Muslims.

The fourth section: It speaks of the criteria for human value in the presence of Allah and the importance of the issue of Taqwa (piety).

The fifth section: It emphasizes that faith is not merely a matter of speech; rather, in addition to heart-felt belief, its effects must manifest in human actions and in Jihad with one's wealth and life.

The sixth section: It discusses that Islam and faith are a great Divine gift for the believers. Instead of considering their acceptance of it as a favor [to the Prophet], they should be exceptionally grateful and thankful for being included in this gift.

Finally, the seventh section: Which is the last part of this Surah, speaks of Allah's knowledge and His awareness of all the hidden secrets of the universe and the actions of humans, which in reality serves as a guarantee for the implementation of all the sections mentioned in this Surah.

The naming of this Surah as "al-Hujurat" is due to its relevance to the fourth verse of this Surah, in which this word is used, and we shall soon learn its commentary.

The Virtue of Reciting this Surah

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it suffices to mention a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ الْحُجُرَاتِ أُعْطِيَ مِنَ الْأَجْرِ عَشْرَ حَسَنَاتٍ بِعَدَدِ مَنْ أَطَاعَ اللَّهَ وَ مَنْ عَصَاهُ

"Whoever recites Surah al-Hujurat will be given ten rewards for every person who has obeyed Allah or disobeyed Him!"

And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ الْحُجُرَاتِ فِي كُلِّ لَيْلَةٍ أَوْ فِي كُلِّ يَوْمٍ كَانَ مِنْ زُوَّارِ مُحَمَّدٍ (ص)

"Whoever recites Surah al-Hujurat every night or every day will be among the visitors of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله."

It is obvious that these numerous rewards, equal to the number of the obedient and the disobedient, are granted if one carefully considers the actions of both groups as reflected in the verses of this Surah, reflects upon them, aligns his path with the former, and separates himself from the latter.

Furthermore, attaining the status of a visitor to the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is contingent upon practically applying the manners mentioned in this Surah regarding His person, for recitation is always a prelude to action.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (1) يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَرْفَعُوا أَصْوَاتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ النَّبِيِّ وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا لَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ أَنْ تَحْبَطَ أَعْمَالُكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ (2) إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَغُضُّونَ أَصْوَاتَهُمْ عِنْدَ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ امْتَحَنَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ لِلتَّقْوى لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (3) إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُنَادُونَكَ مِنْ وَرَاءِ الْحُجُرَاتِ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (4) وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ صَبَرُوا حَتَّى تَخْرُجَ إِلَيْهِمْ لَكَانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (5)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have mentioned several occasions of revelation for the first verse, and different ones for the subsequent verses.

Among the occasions of revelation mentioned for the first verse is that: When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was moving toward "Khaybar," He wanted to appoint someone in His place in "Medina." Umar suggested another person. The above verse was revealed and commanded: Do not precede Allah and the Prophet [1]. Others have said: A group of Muslims would occasionally say, "If such a matter had been revealed about us, it would have been better." The above verse was revealed and said: Do not precede Allah and His Prophet [2].

[1] and [2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6121.

Others have said: The verse refers to the actions of some Muslims who performed some of their acts of worship before the appointed time, and the above verse was revealed to forbid them from such actions [1]. Regarding the second verse, it is said: A group from the tribe of "Bani Tamim" and their nobles entered Medina. When they entered the Mosque of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they raised their voices and shouted from behind the chambers (hujurat) which were the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله residence:

"O Muhammad! Come out to us!"

These noises and impolite expressions distressed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. When He came out, they said, "We have come to boast against you! Permit our 'poet' and 'orator' to recount the glories of the Bani Tamim tribe." The Prophet gave permission.

First, their orator stood up and spoke at length about the imaginary virtues of the Bani Tamim tribe. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to "Thabit ibn Qays" [2], "Respond to them." He stood up and delivered an eloquent sermon in response to them, such that it rendered their sermon ineffective! Then their "poet" stood up and recited poems in praise of this tribe, to which "Hassan ibn Thabit," the famous Muslim poet, gave a sufficient reply.

At this moment, one of the nobles of that tribe named "Aqra'" said: "This man's orator is more powerful than ours, and His poet is more capable than ours, and the tone of their voices is also superior to ours." At this point, to win their hearts, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered that good gifts be given to them. They were influenced by the sum of these events and confessed to the Prophethood of the Prophet. The verses under discussion refer to their noise behind the house of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Another occasion of revelation is mentioned which relates to both the first verse and the subsequent verses: In the ninth year of the Hijra, which was the "Year of Delegations" (Am al-Wufud)—the year when various delegations from tribes came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to embrace Islam or make treaties—when the representatives of the Bani Tamim tribe came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Abu Bakr suggested to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that "Qa'qa'" (one of the nobles of the tribe) be made their leader, while Umar suggested that "Aqra' ibn Habis" (another individual from that tribe) be made leader. Here, Abu Bakr said to Umar: "Did you want to oppose me?" Umar said: "I never intended to oppose you." At this moment, the voices of both were raised in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The above verses were revealed, meaning: neither precede the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in affairs, nor make noise in the presence of the Prophet's house [1].

[1] Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6121; Tafsir Fi Zilal al-Quran, Vol. 7, p. 524; and Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 4, p. 206 onwards (with variations) have narrated this story (this Hadith also appears in Sahih Bukhari, Part 6, p. 172, in the commentary of Surah al-Hujurat).

[2] "Thabit ibn Qays" was the orator of the Ansar and the orator of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, just as "Hassan" was the poet of His Eminence (Usd al-Ghabah, Vol. 1, p. 229).

Commentary

Etiquette in the Presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله

As we noted in the content of the Surah, a series of important ethical discussions and disciplinary instructions were revealed in this Surah, making it worthy of the name "Surah of Ethics." In the verses under discussion at the beginning of the Surah, two parts of these instructions are mentioned:

First, not taking precedence over Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and second, not making noise or a commotion in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

He then says: "O you who have believed, do not put [yourselves] before Allah and His Messenger but fear Allah. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

The meaning of not putting anything before Allah and the Prophet is not preceding them in affairs and abandoning haste and hurry in the face of the command of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Although some commentators have sought to limit the concept of the verse to performing acts of worship before their time, or speaking before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله speaks and the like, it is clear that the verse has a broad and expansive meaning and includes any kind of preceding in any program [1]. The responsibility of the discipline of "followers" toward "leaders"—especially a great Divine leader—requires that they do not precede them in any action, speech, or plan, and that they do not rush or hurry.

Of course, this does not mean that if they have a suggestion or consultation, they should not provide it to the Divine leader; rather, the intention is to avoid getting ahead, making decisions, and acting before their approval. One should not even ask questions or discuss matters more than necessary; one must let the leader raise the issues at the appropriate time—especially an infallible leader who neglects nothing. Furthermore, if someone asks Him a question, others should not take the lead and answer the question hastily. In fact, all these meanings are gathered in the concept of the verse.

[1] "لَا تُقَدِّمُوا" is in the form of a transitive verb, and its object is omitted, being implied as: "Do not put [any matter] before Allah and His Messenger." Some have also considered the possibility that this verb here is intransitive, meaning "Do not go forward before Allah..." Although these two interpretations differ in terms of grammatical principles, they do not differ in meaning and result; in any case, the intention is that they should not precede Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in anything.

The next verse points to the second instruction, saying: "O you who have believed, do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet or be loud to Him in speech like the loudness of some of you to others, lest your deeds become worthless while you perceive not" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَرْفَعُوا أَصْوَاتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ النَّبِيِّ وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا لَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ أَنْ تَحْبَطَ أَعْمَالُكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ).

The first phrase (لَا تَرْفَعُوا أَصْوَاتَكُمْ ...) indicates that one should not make their voice louder than the voice of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which is itself a type of impoliteness in His blessed presence. Let alone the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, this act in front of parents, a master, or a teacher is also contrary to respect and etiquette.

However, the phrase (وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا لَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ ...) might be an emphasis on the same meaning as the first phrase, or it might point to a new point: abandoning the address of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله with the phrase "O Muhammad" and replacing it with "O Messenger of Allah."

Yet, a group of commentators have said regarding the difference between these two phrases: The first phrase refers to when people are speaking with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, where they should not raise their voices above His. The second phrase relates to when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is silent and they are speaking in His presence; in this state too, they should not raise their voices too high.

There is no obstacle to combining this meaning with the previous one, and it is consistent with the occasion of revelation. In any case, the appearance of the verse more likely suggests that it expresses two different points.

It is obvious that if such acts are intended to insult the lofty status of Prophethood, they cause disbelief (kufr), and without that intent, they are a nuisance and a sin.

In the first case, the cause of the nullification (habt) of deeds is clear, because disbelief causes the nullification of the reward of good deeds.

In the second case, there is also no obstacle to such an ugly act causing the destruction of the reward of many deeds. We have previously stated in the discussion of "Habt" that the destruction of the reward of some deeds due to certain specific sins is possible, just as the destruction of the effect of some sins by righteous deeds is certain. There are numerous proofs in the verses of the Quran or Islamic narrations for this meaning, even if this has not been established as a general law for all "good deeds" and "sins." However, regarding some important "good deeds" and "sins," there are transmitted proofs, and there is no rational proof against it [1].

In a narration, it is stated: When the above verse was revealed, "Thabit ibn Qays" (the orator of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله), who had a resonant voice, said: "It was I who raised my voice above the voice of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and spoke loudly in His presence; my deeds are destroyed, and I am of the people of the Fire!"

This reached the ears of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; He said: "It is not so; he is of the people of Paradise" (because he did this while delivering a sermon for the believers or against the opponents, which was the fulfillment of an Islamic duty) [1], just as Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib also, in the Battle of "Hunayn," by the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, called the fugitives to return with a loud voice.

[1] For more explanation regarding the issue of "Habt," please study Vol. 2, p. 70 (under verse 217 of Surah al-Baqarah).

The next verse, for further emphasis on this subject, describes the reward of those who act upon this Divine instruction and observe discipline and etiquette before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Indeed, those who lower their voices before the Messenger of Allah—they are the ones whose hearts Allah has tested for righteousness. For them is forgiveness and a great reward" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَغُضُّونَ أَصْوَاتَهُمْ عِنْدَ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ امْتَحَنَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ لِلتَّقْوى لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ) [2].

"Yaghuddun" is from the root "ghadd," meaning to lower or shorten the gaze or voice, and its opposite is to stare or to raise the voice.

"Imtahana" is from the root "imtihan," which originally meant melting gold to remove its impurities. Sometimes it has also meant "stretching leather," but later it came to be used in the sense of testing, like the verse under discussion—a test whose result is the purity of the heart and its expansion for the acceptance of Taqwa.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verse, the term "Nabi" (Prophet) was used, and here the term "Rasul Allah" (Messenger of Allah) is used. Both seem to point to the fact that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has nothing of His own; He is the envoy of Allah and His messenger. Disrespect toward Him is disrespect toward Allah, and observing etiquette toward Him is observing it toward Allah.

Additionally, the term "maghfirah" (forgiveness) in its indefinite form is for magnification and importance, meaning Allah grants them a complete and great forgiveness. After being purified from sin, He grants them a great reward, because first the washing away of sin is mentioned, then the enjoyment of the great Divine reward.

[1] Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 130—this Hadith, with slight variations in wording, appears in many works of commentators and traditionists, including Nur al-Thaqalayn, Sahih Bukhari, Tafsir Fi Zilal, and Maraghi.

[2] The "Lam" in "lil-Taqwa" is actually the "Lam of purpose" (lam al-ghayah), not the "Lam of cause" (lam al-illah), meaning He makes their hearts pure and ready for the acceptance of Taqwa, because if the heart is not purified and cleansed of pollutions, true Taqwa will not take residence in it.

The next verse, for further emphasis, points to the ignorance and lack of wisdom of those who cast this Divine instruction behind them, saying: "Indeed, those who call as to you from behind the chambers—most of them do not use reason" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُنَادُونَكَ مِنْ وَرَاءِ الْحُجُرَاتِ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ).

What kind of reason is it that a person does not observe etiquette before the greatest Divine ambassador, and with a loud and impolite voice, like the Arabs of "Bani Tamim," comes behind the house of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and shouts: "O Muhammad! O Muhammad! Come out to us!" and thereby annoys and harasses that center of the Lord's mercy and affection?

In principle, the more the level of a person's reason and wisdom rises, the more his etiquette increases, because he better understands "values" and "anti-values." For this reason, impoliteness is always a sign of lack of wisdom, or in other words, impoliteness is the work of an animal and etiquette is the work of a human.

The expression "أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ" (most of them do not understand) is either because "most" in the Arabic language sometimes means "all," used to observe caution and etiquette so that even if one person were an exception, his right would not be lost—as if Allah says with this expression: "I, who am your Lord and have encompassing knowledge of everything, observe etiquette when speaking, so why do you not?"

Or it is because there were truly wise individuals among them who raised their voices due to lack of attention or habitual custom; the Quran warns them through this path to use their intellect and thought and not to forget etiquette.

"Hujurat" is the plural of "hujrah" (chamber), referring here to the multiple rooms prepared next to the Prophet's Mosque for His wives. Originally it is from the root "hajr," meaning "to prevent," because a "hujrah" prevents others from entering the privacy of a person's life. The expression "wara'" here means "outside," from whichever side it may be, because the doors of the Prophet's chambers opened into the Mosque, and ignorant and hasty individuals would sometimes come before the door of the chamber and shout "O Muhammad!" The Quran forbids them from this act.

In the last verse under discussion, to complete this meaning, it adds: "And if they had been patient until you could come out to them, it would have been better for them" (وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ صَبَرُوا حَتَّى تَخْرُجَ إِلَيْهِمْ لَكَانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ).

It is true that haste and hurry sometimes cause a person to reach his goal sooner, but patience and endurance in such a position are a source of mercy, forgiveness, and a great reward, and certainly, this is superior to that.

And since some had unknowingly committed such an act previously and would naturally be terrified by the revelation of this Divine instruction, the Quran also gives them the glad tidings that if they repent, they will be included in Allah's mercy. Therefore, at the end of the verse, it says: "And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Key Points

1- Etiquette is the Greatest Asset

In Islam, great importance has been given to the issue of observing manners and interacting with respect and etiquette toward everyone and every group. As an example, a few Hadiths are mentioned here:

  1. Ali عليه السلام says: الْآدَابُ حُلَلٌ مُجَدَّدَةٌ "Observing etiquette is like a magnificent, decorative, and new garment" [1]. And elsewhere He says: الْأَدَبُ يُغْنِي عَنِ الْحَسَبِ "Etiquette makes a person independent of the glories of fathers and ancestors" [2].

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "There are five things which, if not present in someone, he will not have many enjoyable qualities!" It was asked: "And what are they, O son of the Messenger of Allah?" He said: "Religion, intellect, modesty, good character, and good etiquette" [1].

And also in another Hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read that He said: "The arrogant person should never hope for good praise, nor the deceiver for many friends, nor the ill-mannered for honor" [2].

For this reason, when we examine the lives of the great leaders of Islam, we see that they observed the most precise points regarding etiquette even with young individuals.

In principle, religion is a collection of manners: etiquette toward Allah, etiquette toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Leaders عليه السلام, etiquette toward the master and teacher, parents, and the scholar and scientist.

Even a careful look at the verses of the Holy Quran shows that Allah, with His station of greatness, when He speaks to His servants, observes etiquette completely! Where this is the case, the duty of the people toward Allah and His Prophet is clear.

In a Hadith, we read: "When the opening verses of Surah al-Mu'minun were revealed and commanded a series of Islamic manners to them—including the issue of humility (khushu') in prayer—the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who previously would occasionally look toward the sky during prayer, no longer raised His head and constantly looked at the ground" [1].

Regarding the Prophet of Allah, this subject is so important that the Quran explicitly says in the above verses that raising the voice above the voice of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and creating a commotion in His presence causes the nullification of deeds and the loss of reward.

It is clear that only observing this point before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is not enough; rather, other matters that are similar to raising the voice and commotion in terms of bad etiquette are also forbidden in His presence. In jurisprudential terms, here one must "discard the specific characteristic" (ilgha' al-khususiyyah) and "refine the basis" (tanqih al-manat) and annex similar cases to it.

In verse 63 of Surah al-Nur, we also read: لَا تَجْعَلُوا دُعَاءَ الرَّسُولِ بَيْنَكُمْ كَدُعَاءِ بَعْضِكُمْ بَعْضاً which a group of commentators have interpreted as: "When you call the Prophet, call Him with the etiquette and respect worthy of Him, not like calling one another."

Interestingly, the Quran in the above verses considers observing etiquette before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله a sign of the purity of the heart and its readiness to accept Taqwa, and a cause for forgiveness and a great reward, while it introduces the ill-mannered as being like irrational animals! Some commentators have even expanded the verses under discussion, saying they include lower levels, such as scholars, thinkers, and moral leaders; Muslims are obliged to observe etiquette before them as well.

Of course, regarding the Infallible Imams عليه السلام, this issue is even clearer. Even in narrations that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, we read: "When one of the companions came to them in a state of ritual impurity (janabah), the Imam عليه السلام said without preamble: 'Do you not know that it is not fitting for a person in a state of janabah to enter the houses of the Prophets?!'" [1].

And in another narration, the expression used is: "Indeed, the houses of the Prophets and the children of the Prophets are not entered by one in a state of janabah," which includes both the houses of the Prophets and the houses of their children.

In short, the issue of observing etiquette before the great and the small includes a significant part of Islamic instructions. If we were to discuss them all, we would move beyond the scope of the commentary of the verses. We conclude this discussion here with a Hadith from Imam al-Sajjad Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام in the Treatise on Rights regarding "observing etiquette toward the teacher." He said:

"The right of the one who teaches and educates you is that you should honor him, respect his assembly, listen to his words completely, sit facing him, not raise your voice above his, and whenever someone asks him a question, you should not hasten to answer. Do not speak with anyone in his presence, and do not backbite anyone near him. If someone speaks ill of him behind his back, defend him. Conceal his faults and manifest his virtues. Do not sit with his enemies, and do not treat his friends as enemies. When you do this, the Divine angels will bear witness that you went to him and learned knowledge from him for the sake of Allah, not for the sake of Allah's creation" [2].

[1] Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan; Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, under verse 2 of Surah al-Mu'minun. [2] Mahajjat al-Bayda', Vol. 3, p. 450 (Chapter on the Manners of Companionship and Interaction).

2- Raising the Voice Near the Grave of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله

A group of scholars and commentators have said: Just as the verses under discussion forbid raising the voice near the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله during His lifetime, they also include the period after His passing [1]. If their intention is the inclusion of the wording of the verse, the appearance of the verse is specific to the lifetime of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, because it says: "Do not raise your voices above His voice," and this is while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has a physical life and speaks.

However, if the intention is the basis and philosophy of the ruling, which is clear in such cases and people of understanding discard the specific characteristic, the aforementioned generalization does not seem far-fetched. It is certain that the goal here is to observe etiquette and respect toward the holy presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Therefore, whenever raising the voice near the grave of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is a type of desecration and disrespect, it is undoubtedly not permissible, unless it is in the form of the call to prayer (adhan), recitation of the Quran, delivering a sermon, or the like, which in such cases was not forbidden during the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life nor after His death.

In a Hadith in Usul al-Kafi regarding the event of the passing of Imam al-Hasan al-Mujtaba عليه السلام and the prevention that was carried out by "Aisha" regarding the burial of His Eminence near the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the noises that were raised, we read: Imam al-Husayn عليه السلام argued with the verse "O you who have believed, do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet..." and narrated this sentence from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Indeed, Allah has forbidden regarding the believers when dead what He forbade regarding them when alive" [2].

This Hadith is another proof of the generality of the concept of the verse.

[1] Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 26, p. 125. [2] Usul al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 80.

3- Islamic Discipline in Everything and Everywhere

The issue of management and leadership will never be organized without observing discipline. If those who are under management and leadership act arbitrarily, the fabric of affairs will fall apart, no matter how capable and worthy the leader and commander may be.

Many of the defeats and failures that have afflicted populations, groups, and armies have been from this very path. Muslims too have tasted the bitter taste of violating this instruction many times during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or after Him, the clearest of which was the story of the defeat at Uhud due to the lack of discipline of a small group of warriors.

The Holy Quran has raised this exceptionally important issue in the short phrases of the above verses in a comprehensive and interesting way, saying: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ.

The breadth of the concept of the verse, as we said, is so great that it includes any kind of "preceding" or "lagging" and arbitrary speech and behavior outside the instruction of the leadership.

Despite this, many cases are seen in the history of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life where individuals preceded His command, or lagged behind, and disobeyed it, and were severely blamed and rebuked. Among them:

  1. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله moved for the conquest of Mecca (the eighth year of the Hijra), it was the holy month of Ramadan. A large crowd was with His Eminence, some mounted and some on foot. When He reached the station of "Kura' al-Ghamim," He ordered a vessel of water to be brought and His Eminence صلى الله عليه وآله broke His fast. The companions also broke their fast, but strangely, a group of them preceded the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and were not willing to break their fast and remained on their fast. The Prophet called them 'usat, meaning "the group of sinners" [1].

  2. Another example occurred in the story of the "Farewell Pilgrimage" (Hujjat al-Wada') in the tenth year of the Hijra, where the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered a caller to announce that whoever had not brought a sacrificial animal with them must first perform Umrah and come out of Ihram, then perform the Hajj rituals. As for those who had brought a sacrificial animal with them (and their Hajj was Hajj al-Ifrad), they must remain in their Ihram. Then He added: "If I had not brought a sacrificial camel, I would have completed the Umrah and come out of Ihram." But a group refused to carry out this instruction and said: "How is it possible for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to remain in His Ihram while we come out of Ihram? Is it not ugly for us to go toward the Hajj rituals after performing Umrah while drops of water from the ritual bath (ghusl janabah) are falling from us?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was severely distressed by this violation and lack of discipline and rebuked them strongly [2].

  3. The story of the violation regarding the army of "Usama" on the eve of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله passing is famous. His Eminence ordered the Muslims to prepare for war with the Romans under the command of "Usama ibn Zayd," and told the Muhajirun and Ansar to move with this army. Perhaps He wanted the issues that occurred regarding the matter of the Caliphate at the time of His passing not to be realized, and He even cursed those who violated the army of Usama. However, despite this, a group refused to move on the pretext that "in these specific conditions, we will not leave the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله alone" [1].

  4. The story of the "pen and inkwell" in the final hours of the life of the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is also famous and shocking. It is best that we bring the exact wording of Sahih Muslim here: "When the Messenger of Allah was near death and there were men in the house, among them Umar ibn al-Khattab, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'Come, let me write for you a writing after which you will never go astray.' Umar said: 'Indeed, pain has overcome the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله! (and Allah forbid, He is speaking incoherently!) You have the Quran; the Book of Allah is sufficient for us!' Then the people of the house disagreed and quarreled. Some of them said: 'Bring it so the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله may write for you a writing after which you will never go astray,' while others said what Umar said! When the nonsensical talk and disagreement increased in the presence of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, the Messenger of Allah said: 'Stand up [and leave]!'" [1].

It is noteworthy that "Bukhari" also brought this same Hadith with slight variations in his Sahih [2]. This event is one of the important events of Islamic history that needs much analysis, and this is not the place for its explanation. But in any case, it is considered one of the clearest cases of violation of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله instruction and opposition to the verse under discussion (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ).

The important issue here is that observing this Divine and Islamic discipline requires a spirit of complete submission and acceptance of leadership in all aspects of life and firm faith in the lofty status of the leader.

[1] Many historians and traditionists have narrated this Hadith, including in Vol. 7 of Wasa'il, p. 125 (Chapters on those from whom fasting is valid) (with some abridgment). [2] Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 21, p. 386 (with abridgment). [1] This event is written in many books of Islamic history and is among the important events of Islamic history (for more information, refer to the book al-Muraja'at, Letter 90). [1] Sahih Muslim, Vol. 3, Book of Wills, Hadith 22 (p. 1259). [2] Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 6, Chapter on the Illness of the Prophet and His Passing, p. 11.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن جَآءَكُمْ فَاسِقٌۢ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ أَن تُصِيبُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ فَتُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ (٦)
O you who have faith! If a vicious character brings you some news, verify it, lest you should visit [harm] on some people out of ignorance, and then become regretful for what you have done. (6)
وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ وَزَيَّنَهُۥ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ وَكَرَّهَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ وَٱلْفُسُوقَ وَٱلْعِصْيَانَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلرَّٰشِدُونَ (٧)
Know that the Apostle of Allah is among you. Should he comply with you in many matters, you would surely suffer. But Allah has endeared faith to you and made it appealing in your hearts, and He has made hateful to you faithlessness, transgression and disobedience. It is such who are the right-minded— (7)
فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَنِعْمَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٨)
a grace and blessing from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (8)

Circumstances of Revelation

Regarding the first verse under discussion, two accounts for the circumstances of its revelation have been mentioned in commentaries. Some, like "Tabarsi" in Majma' al-Bayan, have cited both, while others, such as Qurtubi, Nur al-Thaqalayn, and Fi Zilal al-Quran, have limited themselves to one.

The first account, mentioned by most commentators, is that the verse: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ جَاءَكُمْ... was revealed concerning "Walid ibn Uqbah." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had dispatched him to collect Zakat from the "Bani al-Mustaliq" tribe. When the tribespeople heard that the representative of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله was coming, they rushed out joyfully to welcome him. However, because there had been intense hostility between them and Walid during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), he imagined they had come with the intention of killing him.

He returned to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (without investigating this suspicion) and claimed: "They refused to pay the Zakat!" (And we know that refusing to pay Zakat was considered a form of rebellion against the Islamic government; thus, he was claiming they had apostatized).

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became very angry and decided to wage war against them. Then the above verse was revealed (commanding the Muslims that whenever a wicked person brings news, they must investigate it) ^1. Some have added that after Walid's report regarding the apostasy of the Bani al-Mustaliq, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered "Khalid ibn Walid ibn Mughirah" to go to the tribe but told him: "Do not act hastily."

Khalid reached the vicinity of the tribe at night and sent his intelligence officers to investigate. They brought back news that the Bani al-Mustaliq were fully committed to Islam, and they had heard the sound of their Adhan and prayers with their own ears. In the morning, Khalid went to them personally and observed the truth of the informants' words. He returned to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and reported the matter. At this moment, the above verse was revealed, following which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would say:

"Deliberation is from Allah, and haste is from Satan!" ^1

^1: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 132.

Another account of revelation, mentioned only by some commentators, is that the verse was revealed regarding "Mariyah," the wife of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (the mother of Ibrahim). It was reported to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that she had a cousin who visited her from time to time (implying an illicit relationship). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله summoned Ali عليه السلام and said: "My brother! Take this sword; if you find him with Mariyah, kill him."

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said: "O Messenger of Allah! Am I commanded to carry out your order like a 'heated die' [^2], or should the one present see what the one absent does not see? (Should I perform my duty with further investigation?)"

He replied: "No! Act on the basis that the one present sees what the one absent does not see."

^1: Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6131.

[^2]: "Sikkah" (die) in Arabic refers to the tool used to stamp coins, which is heated so that it transfers its design precisely onto the dirham or dinar. This expression means the command should be executed unconditionally and without re-evaluation.

Ali عليه السلام says: "I girded my sword and went to him. I saw him with Mariyah. I drew my sword; he fled and climbed a palm tree, then threw himself down. At that moment, his garment was pulled up, and it became clear that he had no sexual organs at all. I came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and explained the incident. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'Praise be to Allah who keeps evil, impurity, and accusation away from our household.'" ^1

Commentary

Do Not Heed the News of the Wicked!

In the previous verses, the discussion was about the duties of Muslims toward their leader and guide, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and two important commands were given: first, not to precede Allah and His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and second, to observe etiquette when speaking and calling out in his presence.

The verses under discussion continue the duties of the Ummah toward this great leader, stating that when news is brought to him, it must be based on investigation. If a wicked person (fasiq) reports something, it should not be accepted without verification, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله should not be pressured to accept it.

First, He says: "O you who have believed, if there comes to you a disobedient one with information, investigate..." (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ جَاءَكُمْ فَاسِقٌ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوا).

Then, He points to the reason, adding: "Lest you harm a people out of ignorance and become, over what you have done, regretful." (أَنْ تُصِيبُوا قَوْماً بِجَهَالَةٍ فَتُصْبِحُوا عَلَى مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَادِمِينَ).

Just as if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had acted upon the word of Walid ibn Uqbah and fought the Bani al-Mustaliq as an apostate people, a painful tragedy and disaster would have occurred.

From the tone of the following verse, it is understood that a group was insisting on this battle. The Quran says: This is not worthy of you; this is pure ignorance, and its end will be remorse and regret.

A group of scholars of Usul al-Fiqh (Principles of Jurisprudence) have used this verse to argue for the authority of a "solitary report" (khabar al-wahid), because the verse says investigation is necessary for the news of a "wicked person," and the implication (mafhum) is that if a "just person" (adil) gives news, it can be accepted without investigation.

However, many objections have been raised against this argument, the most important of which are two:

First, the above argument depends on accepting the "authority of the implication of a description" (hujjiyat mafhum al-wasf), while it is well-known that the implication of a description is not authoritative ^1. Second, the reason mentioned at the end of the verse is so broad that it includes both the news of the "just" and the "wicked," because acting on any speculative news carries the possibility of regret and remorse.

But both these objections are resolvable. The implication of a description and any other qualification is authoritative in cases where the intention is to state the constraints of an issue and for the purpose of exclusion (ihtiraz). Mentioning this qualification (the qualification of "wicked") in the above verse, according to common linguistic understanding, has no significant benefit other than stating the authority of the news of a just person.

As for the reasoning at the end of the verse, it appears that it does not include every kind of action based on "speculative evidence" (adillah zanniyyah), but rather refers to cases where the action is ignorant, foolish, or senseless, as the verse emphasizes the term "ignorance" (jahalah). We know that most of the evidence relied upon by all rational people in daily life is speculative (such as the apparent meaning of words, the testimony of a witness, the opinion of an expert, the word of a possessor of property, and the like).

Clearly, none of these are considered ignorant or foolish, and if they occasionally do not correspond to reality, the issue of regret does not arise because it is a universal and common path.

In any case, in our view, this verse is among the "firm verses" (muhkam) that indicate the "authority of a solitary report" even in "subject matters" (mawdu'at), and there are extensive discussions in this field that are not appropriate to detail here.

Furthermore, it cannot be denied that the issue of relying on reliable reports forms the basis of history and human life, such that if the authority of the news of a just or reliable person were removed from human societies, many scientific heritages of the past, information regarding human societies, and even many matters we deal with today in our society would be completely eliminated. Not only would man regress, but the wheels of his current life would also stop turning.

Therefore, the consensus of all rational people is upon its authority, and the Holy Legislator has endorsed it both "verbally" and "practically."

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 132; and in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, this circumstance of revelation is mentioned in more detail (Vol. 5, p. 81).

^1: Some have imagined that this is a case of the "implication of a condition" (mafhum al-shart), and the implication of a condition is authoritative, whereas it has no relation to the implication of a condition. Additionally, the conditional sentence here is for stating the subject matter, and we know that in such cases, the "conditional sentence" also lacks an implication (reflect on this).

However, just as the authority of a reliable solitary report brings order to life, relying on unreliable reports is very dangerous and causes the disintegration of the social order. It brings many calamities, endangers the reputation and rights of individuals, and leads man astray. In the beautiful expression of the Quran in the verse under discussion, it will ultimately be a cause of remorse and regret.

It is also noteworthy that fabricating false news and relying on unreliable reports is one of the ancient weapons of tyrannical and colonial systems, through which they create a false atmosphere and, by deceiving and misleading unaware people, lead them astray and plunder their wealth.

If Muslims strictly follow this divine command mentioned in this verse and do not accept the news of the wicked without investigation and verification, they will be protected from these great calamities.

Another point to consider is that the important issue is the "trustworthiness and reliability of the news itself." However, sometimes this trust is obtained through confidence in the "person reporting," and sometimes from other external clues. Therefore, in some cases, even though the speaker is wicked, we gain confidence in his report.

Thus, however this trust and confidence is obtained—whether through the justice, piety, and honesty of the speaker, or through external clues—it is valid for us. The practice of rational people (sirat al-uqala), which has been endorsed by Islamic law, is also based on this.

For this reason, we see in Islamic jurisprudence that many reports whose chains of narration are weak are used as a criterion for action because they have been "acted upon by the majority" (mashhur), who became aware of the report's correctness through clues, and they issue fatwas accordingly.

Conversely, sometimes reports are narrated where the speaker is a reliable person, but external clues make us suspicious of that report; here, we have no choice but to set it aside, even though the speaker is a just and reliable person.

Therefore, the criterion everywhere is confidence in the "news" itself, although the justice and honesty of the narrator is often the means for this confidence, but it is not a universal law (reflect on this).

In the next verse, to emphasize the important matter mentioned in the previous verse, it adds: "And know that among you is the Messenger of Allah. If he were to obey you in much of the matter, you would be in difficulty..." (وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ) ^1. This sentence, as a group of commentators has said, shows that after Walid's news about the apostasy of the Bani al-Mustaliq, a group of simple-minded and superficial Muslims pressured the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to take military action against the said tribe.

The Quran says: "It is part of your good fortune that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله is among you, and the connection with the world of revelation is established through him; whenever deviant paths appear among you, He informs you through this means."

"But He is the leader; do not expect Him to obey you or take orders from you. He is more merciful to you than anyone else; do not pressure Him to impose your own thoughts, for this is to your detriment."

In the continuation of the verse, it points to another of Allah's great blessings to the believers, saying: "But Allah has endeared to you faith and has made it pleasing in your hearts" (وَلَكِنَّ اللَّهَ حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْإِيمَانَ وَزَيَّنَهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ).

"And has made hateful to you disbelief, defiance and disobedience" (وَكَرَّهَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْكُفْرَ وَالْفُسُوقَ وَالْعِصْيَانَ).

In reality, these expressions are a subtle reference to the "Law of Grace" (Lutf), specifically "Existential Grace" (Lutf Takwini).

To explain: when a Wise Being wants a task to be realized, He provides its grounds from every aspect. This principle is perfectly applicable to the guidance of humans.

God wants all humans—without being subjected to a program of compulsion—to follow the path of truth by their own will and volition. Therefore, on one hand, He sends messengers and prophets with heavenly books, and on the other hand, He makes "faith" beloved to humans, igniting the fire of love for truth-seeking and truth-finding within their souls, and creating a sense of hatred and aversion toward disbelief, oppression, hypocrisy, and sin in their hearts.

In this way, every human being naturally desires faith, purity, and piety, and loathes disbelief and sin.

However, it is entirely possible that in later stages, this pure water poured from the heaven of creation into the existence of humans may lose its clarity due to contact with polluted environments and take on the repulsive smell of sin, disbelief, and disobedience.

This innate gift invites humans to follow the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and not to precede Him.

It is also necessary to remind that the content of this verse in no way contradicts the issue of "consultation" (shura), because the goal of "shura" is for everyone to express their opinion, but the final decision rests with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, as is also understood from the verse of "Shura."

In other words: Shura is one thing, and imposing one's thought and opinion is another; the verse under discussion negates the imposition of thought, not consultation.

Regarding what is meant by "fusuq" (defiance/wickedness) in the above verse, some have interpreted it as "lying," but given the breadth of its linguistic meaning and the absence of a constraint in the verse, it includes every kind of sin and departure from obedience. Therefore, the expression "isyan" (disobedience) after it serves as an emphasis, just as the phrase "He made it pleasing in your hearts" (زَيَّنَهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ) is an emphasis on the phrase "He endeared to you faith" (حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْإِيمَانَ).

Some consider "fusuq" to refer to "major sins," while they consider "isyan" to be more general, but there is no evidence for this distinction either.

In any case, at the end of the verse, it states as a general and universal rule:

"Those are the [rightly] guided" (أُولَئِكَ هُمُ الرَّاشِدُونَ).

That is, if you preserve this divine gift (love for faith and hatred for disbelief and sin) and do not pollute this purity and clarity of nature (fitrah), growth and guidance undoubtedly await you.

It is noteworthy that the previous sentences were addressed to the believers, but this sentence mentions them in the third person. This change in expression is apparently to show that this ruling is not exclusive to the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but is a universal law: whoever preserves the purity of their nature in any age or time is among the people of salvation and guidance.

The last verse under discussion clarifies the reality that this endearment of faith and the loathing of disbelief and disobedience are among the great divine blessings upon mankind.

It says: "[It is] as bounty from Allah and favor. And Allah is Knowing and Wise" (فَضْلاً مِنَ اللَّهِ وَنِعْمَةً وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ) ^1. His knowledge and wisdom necessitate that He creates the factors of growth and happiness within you, harmonizes and completes them with the call of the prophets, and finally brings you to the intended destination.

It appears that "bounty" (fadl) and "favor" (ni'mah) both refer to one reality: the gifts bestowed by Allah upon His servants. However, it is called "bounty" because Allah does not need it, and "favor" because the servants are in need of it; thus, they are like two sides of the same coin.

Undoubtedly, Allah's knowledge of the servants' needs and His wisdom regarding the perfection and nurturing of creatures necessitate that He grants them these great spiritual blessings—namely, the endearment of faith and the loathing of disbelief and disobedience.

^1: "Fadlan wa ni'matan" is either an "object of purpose" (maf'ul la-ajlih) for "He endeared to you faith..." or an "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq) for a hidden verb, estimated as "He bestowed bounty as a bounty and favored as a favor."

Key Points

1- "Divine Guidance" and "Freedom of Will"

The above verses provide a clear depiction of Islam's perspective on the issue of "determinism and free will" and "guidance and misguidance," for they clearly reveal that Allah's work is to provide the grounds for growth and guidance.

On one hand, He places the "Messenger of Allah" صلى الله عليه وآله among the people and reveals the "Quran," which is a program of guidance and light. On the other hand, He places "love for faith" and "aversion and hatred for disbelief and disobedience" as a foundation within the souls. But ultimately, He leaves the decision-making to the people themselves and legislates duties in this regard.

According to the above verses, the love for faith and hatred for disbelief exist in the hearts of all humans without exception, and if some lack these grounds, it is due to wrong upbringing and their own actions. Allah has not created "love for disobedience" or "hatred for faith" in anyone's heart.

2- Leadership and Obedience

These verses once again emphasize that the existence of a "divine leader" is necessary for the growth and development of a community, provided that he is "obeyed" and not "obedient" to his followers. They should place his command upon their eyes, not pressure him to execute their limited goals and thoughts.

This principle is established not only for divine leaders but also in the matter of "management" and "command" everywhere; this principle must govern.

The governance of this principle means neither the autocracy of leaders nor the abandonment of consultation, as was also mentioned above.

3- Faith is a Kind of "Love," Not Just "Intellectual Perception"

These verses also hint at the reality that faith is a kind of intense divine and spiritual affection, even if it stems from intellectual arguments. Thus, in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he was asked: "Are love and hatred part of faith?" He replied:

"Is faith anything but love and hatred?! Then he recited this verse: 'Allah has endeared to you faith and has made it pleasing in your hearts and has made hateful to you disbelief, defiance and disobedience. Those are the [rightly] guided.'" ^1

In another tradition from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is stated:

"Is religion anything but love?!" Then he argued using several verses from the Holy Quran, including the verse under discussion, and added at the end:

"Religion is love, and love is religion!" ^1

But undoubtedly, this love—as we have said—must also be nourished and made fruitful by roots of reasoning and logic.

^1: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Love for Allah and Hatred for Allah, Hadith 5. ^1: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, pp. 83 and 84.

Ayahs

وَإِن طَآئِفَتَانِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱقْتَتَلُوا۟ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ فَإِنۢ بَغَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ فَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّتِى تَبْغِى حَتَّىٰ تَفِىٓءَ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن فَآءَتْ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَأَقْسِطُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ (٩)
If two groups of the faithful fight one another, make peace between them. But if one party of them aggresses against the other, fight the one which aggresses until it returns to Allah’s ordinance. Then, if it returns, make peace between them fairly, and do justice. Indeed Allah loves the just. (9)
إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (١٠)
The faithful are indeed brothers. Therefore, make peace between your brothers and be wary of Allah, so that you may receive [His] mercy. (10)

Circumstances of Revelation

Regarding the circumstances of the revelation of these verses, it is mentioned that a dispute arose between the two tribes of "Aws" and "Khazraj" (the two famous tribes of Medina), which led to a group from both sides attacking one another, striking each other with sticks and shoes! (The above verse was revealed and taught the Muslims how to deal with such incidents) [^1]. Others have said: two men from the "Ansar" had a feud and dispute; one said to the other, "I will take my right from you by force, for my tribe is large in number!" The other said, "We shall go to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله for judgment." The first person refused, and the conflict escalated until groups from both tribes attacked each other with hands, shoes, and even swords. The above verses were revealed (clarifying the duty of Muslims in the face of such disputes) [^2].

Commentary

Believers are Brothers to One Another

The Quran here states a general and universal law for all times and places: "And if two factions among the believers should fight, then make settlement between them" (وَ إِنْ طائِفَتانِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ اقتَتَلُوا فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَهُما) [^3]. It is true that "iqtatalu" is derived from "qital," meaning war, but the context here testifies that it includes any kind of dispute or conflict, even if it has not reached the stage of war and combat; some of the circumstances of revelation narrated for the verse also support this meaning.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 132. [^2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6136. [^3]: Although "ta'ifatan" (two factions) is dual, its verb "iqtatalu" appears in the plural form, because each faction is composed of a group of individuals.

Rather, it can be said that if the grounds for conflict and dispute are prepared—for instance, verbal arguments and tensions that serve as a prelude to bloody conflicts—taking action for reconciliation is mandatory according to this verse, as this meaning can be derived from the verse through the principle of "discarding the specificities" (ilqa' al-khususiyyah).

In any case, it is an absolute duty for all Muslims to prevent disputes, conflicts, and bloodshed among Muslims, and to consider themselves responsible in this regard, rather than passing by these scenes as indifferent spectators like some uninformed individuals.

This is the first duty of believers when encountering such scenes.

Then, it describes the second duty as follows: "And if one of them oppresses the other and does not submit to the proposal of peace, then fight against the one that oppresses until it returns to the ordinance of Allah" (فَإِنْ بَغَتْ إِحْداهُما عَلَى الْأُخْرى‌ فَقاتِلُوا الَّتِي تَبْغِي حَتَّى تَفِي‌ءَ إِلى‌ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ‌).

It is obvious that if the blood of the oppressive and transgressing faction is shed in this process, it is on their own heads, and so to speak, their blood is "wasted" (hadar), even though they are Muslims, for the premise is that the dispute occurred between two factions of believers.

In this way, Islam considers the prevention of oppression and tyranny necessary, even at the cost of war with the oppressor, and deems the price of implementing justice higher than even the blood of Muslims—and this is in the event that the issue cannot be resolved through peaceful means.

Then, it proceeds to state the third command, saying: "And if it returns (submits to the command of Allah and the grounds for peace are prepared), then make settlement between them in justice" (فَإِنْ فاءَتْ فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَهُما بِالْعَدْلِ‌).

This means one should not be content merely with breaking the power of the oppressive faction; rather, this combat must pave the way for peace and be a prelude to uprooting the causes of dispute and conflict. Otherwise, after a short or long period, once the oppressor feels capable again, he will rise and resume the conflict.

Some commentators have inferred from the expression "bi-l-'adl" (in justice) that if a right has been trampled upon between these two groups, or blood has been shed that was the source of the conflict, that too must be rectified; otherwise, it would not be "reconciliation in justice" [^1]. And since group inclinations sometimes sway individuals toward one of the "two litigating factions" during judgment and arbitration, violating the neutrality of the arbiters, the Quran warns Muslims in the fourth and final command:

"And act justly; indeed, Allah loves those who act justly" (وَ أَقْسِطُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ‌) [^2].

In the following verse, to emphasize this matter and state its cause, it adds: "The believers are but brothers, so make settlement between your brothers" (إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ‌).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 18, p. 342. [^2]: "Muqsitin" is from the root "qist," which originally means a fair share. When used as a simple triliteral verb (qasata), it means to act unjustly and take another's fair share. However, when used in the increased triliteral form (aqsata), it means justice and giving everyone their fair share. Regarding whether qist and 'adl have the same meaning or differ, we have provided an explanation in Vol. 6, p. 143 (under verse 29 of Surah Al-A'raf).

To the same extent that you strive and endeavor to create peace between two biological brothers, you must also enter the field of action seriously and decisively to establish peace among conflicting believers.

What a beautiful and captivating expression! That all believers are brothers to one another, and the dispute and conflict between them is called a conflict between brothers, which must quickly give way to peace and sincerity.

And since "relationships" often replace "regulations" in such matters, He warns once more and adds at the end of the verse: "And fear Allah that you may receive mercy" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ‌).

In this way, one of the most important social responsibilities of Muslims toward one another and in the implementation of social justice in all its dimensions becomes clear.

Key Points

1- Conditions for Fighting the People of Rebellion (Bughat)

In Islamic jurisprudence, in the Book of Jihad, there is a discussion titled "Fighting the People of Rebellion" (Qital Ahl al-Baghy), referring to oppressors who rise against a just Imam and the true leader of the Muslims. There are numerous rulings for them mentioned in that chapter.

However, the discussion raised in the above verse is a different matter; it concerns disputes and conflicts that occur between two groups of believers, where there is neither an uprising against an infallible Imam nor an uprising against a righteous Islamic government. Although some jurists or commentators have tried to use this verse for the former issue as well.

But as "Fadil Miqdad" says in Kanz al-'Irfan, this reasoning is an error [^1], because rising against an infallible Imam leads to disbelief (kufr), whereas a dispute between believers only leads to transgression (fisq), not disbelief. Therefore, the Holy Quran in the above verses has called both groups "believers" and "religious brothers" of one another. Thus, the rulings of "Ahl al-Baghy" cannot be generalized to such individuals.

Unfortunately, we did not find a discussion in jurisprudence regarding the rulings of this group, but what can be inferred from the above verse, combined with other clues—especially the indications found in the chapters on "Enjoining the Good and Forbidding the Evil"—are the following "rulings":

A- Reconciliation between conflicting groups of Muslims is a communal obligation (wajib kifa'i).

B- To achieve this, one must first start with simpler stages and observe the principle of "the easiest, then the next easiest" (al-ashal fa-l-ashal). However, if it does not prove effective, armed struggle and war are also permissible, rather necessary.

C- The blood of the rebels and aggressors shed in this path and the property of theirs that is destroyed is "wasted" (hadar), because it occurred by the decree of Sharia and the fulfillment of a mandatory duty, and the principle in such cases is non-liability ('adam al-daman).

D- In the stages of reconciliation through dialogue, the permission of the Sharia judge is not required. However, in the stage of severe action, especially where it leads to bloodshed, it is not permissible without the permission of the Islamic government and the Sharia judge, except in cases where access is impossible, in which case the "just believers" ('udul al-mu'minin) and informed individuals make the decision.

E- If the rebellious and oppressive faction sheds the blood of the "reconciling group" or

[^1]: Kanz al-'Irfan fi Fiqh al-Qur'an, "Book of Jihad," "Chapter on Other Types of Jihad," Vol. 1, p. 386.

destroys property, they are liable according to Sharia, and in the case of intentional murder, the ruling of retaliation (qisas) applies. Likewise, regarding the blood shed from the oppressed faction and the property destroyed, the rulings of "liability" (daman) and "retaliation" (qisas) are established. The idea inferred from the words of some—that after peace occurs, the rebellious faction has no responsibility for the blood and property wasted because it was not mentioned in the verse under discussion—is incorrect. The verse is not intended to state all these details; rather, the reference in such matters is the other principles and rules found in the chapters on retaliation and destruction of property (itlaf).

F- Since the goal of this combat and war is to compel the oppressive faction to accept the truth, the issue of prisoners of war and spoils will not be raised, for the premise is that both groups are Muslims. However, temporary imprisonment to extinguish the fire of conflict is not prohibited, but after peace, the prisoners must be released immediately.

G- Sometimes it happens that both sides of the dispute are rebels and oppressors; they have killed a group from the other tribe and taken property, and the others have done the same to the first tribe, without sufficing with the amount necessary for defense—whether both commit rebellion and oppression to the same extent or one more and the other less.

Of course, the ruling for this case is not explicitly mentioned in the Holy Quran, but its ruling can be understood through the principle of "discarding the specificities" from the verse under discussion: that the duty of Muslims is to reconcile both, and if they do not submit to peace, to fight both until they yield to the Divine command. The rulings mentioned above regarding the rebel and aggressor apply to both.

At the end of this discourse, we emphasize again that the ruling for these rebels is separate from those who rise against an infallible Imam or a just Islamic government; the latter group has harsher and more severe rulings mentioned in Islamic jurisprudence in the "Book of Jihad."

2- The Importance of Islamic Brotherhood

The phrase "إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ" (The believers are but brothers) mentioned in the above verses is one of the fundamental and deep-rooted Islamic slogans—a slogan that is very captivating, profound, effective, and meaningful.

When others want to express great affection for those of their own ideology, they refer to them as "comrades," but Islam has raised the level of the bond of friendship among Muslims so high that it presents it as the closest bond between two humans, and moreover, a bond based on equality and parity: the affection of "two brothers" toward one another.

Based on this important Islamic principle, Muslims of every race and tribe, and of every language and age, feel a deep sense of brotherhood with one another, even if one lives in the East of the world and the other in the West.

In the "Hajj" ceremonies, where Muslims from all parts of the world gather in that center of Monotheism, this close affection, bond, and solidarity are completely palpable, and it is a scene of the practical realization of this important Islamic law.

In other words, Islam considers all Muslims as members of one family and addresses them all as brothers and sisters—not only in words and slogans but also in action and mutual commitments.

In Islamic narrations, this issue has been emphasized tremendously, and its practical aspects have been specifically presented. As an example, we review a few of the following profound hadiths:

1- In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "A Muslim is the brother of a Muslim; he never oppresses him, he does not withhold his help, and he does not leave him alone in the face of incidents." [^1]

2- In another hadith narrated from the same Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The likeness of two religious brothers is like two hands, one of which washes the other!" (They have perfect cooperation with each other and cleanse each other's faults). [^2]

3- Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "A believer is the brother of a believer; they are like a single body: if one part of it suffers, the rest of the body feels that pain, and their souls are from a single soul." [^3]

4- In another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read: "A believer is the brother of a believer, his eye and his guide; he never betrays him, he does not oppress him, he does not deceive him, and he does not make a promise to him and then break it." [^4]

[^1]: Al-Mahajjat al-Bayda', Vol. 3, p. 332 (Book of the Etiquettes of Companionship and Socializing, Chapter 2). [^2]: Ibid, p. 319. [^3] and [^4]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 133 (Chapter on the Brotherhood of Believers with One Another, Hadiths 3 and 4).

In famous Islamic sources of hadith, many narrations have been recorded regarding the rights of a believer over his Muslim brother, the various types of rights believers have over one another, the reward for visiting believer brothers, shaking hands, embracing, remembering them, making their hearts joyful, and especially fulfilling the needs of believers and striving to achieve these requests, removing grief from hearts, feeding, clothing, and honoring and respecting them. Important sections of these can be studied in Usul al-Kafi in various chapters under the aforementioned titles.

5- At the end of this discussion, we refer to a narration from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the thirty rights of a believer over his believer brother, which is among the most comprehensive narrations in this field:

The Messenger of Allah (ص) said: "A Muslim has thirty rights over his brother, from which he is not absolved except by fulfilling them or by his Muslim brother's forgiveness:

He should forgive his lapses, be merciful to him in his distress, keep his secrets hidden, compensate for his mistakes, accept his excuse, defend him against backbiters, always be his well-wisher, safeguard his friendship, observe his covenant, visit him when he is ill, and attend his funeral when he dies.

He should accept his invitation, accept his gift, repay his favor, thank him for his blessing, strive to help him, protect his honor, fulfill his need, intercede for his request, and say 'bless you' when he sneezes.

He should guide him when he is lost, respond to his greeting, speak well of his words, make his favors good, testify to his oaths, love his friend and not be his enemy, and strive to help him whether he is an oppressor or oppressed: as for helping him when he is an oppressor, it is by restraining him from his oppression; and as for helping him when he is oppressed, it is by assisting him in taking his right.

He should not leave him alone in the face of incidents, he should love for him the good he loves for himself, and hate for him the evil he hates for himself." [^1]

In any case, one of the rights of Muslims over one another is the issue of helping and "reconciling between people" (islah dhat al-bayn) in the manner mentioned in the above verses and narrations (regarding the reconciliation between people, we had another discussion in Vol. 7, p. 83 onwards, under verse 1 of Surah Al-Anfal).

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 74, p. 236.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَسْخَرْ قَوْمٌ مِّن قَوْمٍ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونُوا۟ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَلَا نِسَآءٌ مِّن نِّسَآءٍ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُنَّ ۖ وَلَا تَلْمِزُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَلَا تَنَابَزُوا۟ بِٱلْأَلْقَـٰبِ ۖ بِئْسَ ٱلِٱسْمُ ٱلْفُسُوقُ بَعْدَ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَتُبْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (١١)
O you who have faith! Let not any people ridicule another people: it may be that they are better than they are; nor let women [ridicule] women: it may be that they are better than they are. And do not defame one another, nor insult one another by [calling] nicknames. How evil are profane names subsequent to faith! As for those who are not penitent [of their past conduct]—such are the wrongdoers. (11)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلظَّنِّ إِنَّ بَعْضَ ٱلظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ ۖ وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا۟ وَلَا يَغْتَب بَّعْضُكُم بَعْضًا ۚ أَيُحِبُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن يَأْكُلَ لَحْمَ أَخِيهِ مَيْتًا فَكَرِهْتُمُوهُ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢)
O you who have faith! Avoid much suspicion; indeed some suspicions are sins. And do not spy on one another or backbite. Will any of you love to eat the flesh of his dead brother? You would hate it. Be wary of Allah; indeed Allah is all-clement, all-merciful. (12)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have cited various occasions of revelation for these verses, including:

The phrase "Let not a people ridicule [another] people" was revealed regarding Thabit ibn Qays (the orator of the Prophet ﷺ), who was hard of hearing. When he entered the mosque, people would make room for him near the Prophet ﷺ so he could hear his words. One day, he entered the mosque while people had finished their prayers and taken their seats. He began pushing through the crowd, saying, "Make room! Make room!" until he reached a man who told him, "Sit right here!" Thabit sat behind him but was angry. When it became light, Thabit asked the man, "Who are you?"

He mentioned his name, saying, "I am so-and-so." Thabit replied, "The son of such-and-such woman?!" mentioning his mother by a derogatory title used during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah). The man felt ashamed and lowered his head. Then the verse was revealed, forbidding Muslims from such ugly actions.

It is also said that "nor let women ridicule [other] women" was revealed regarding Umm Salama, whom some of the Prophet's ﷺ wives ridiculed because of a specific garment she wore or because of her short stature. The verse was revealed to restrain them from this act.

Furthermore, it is said that the phrase "and do not backbite one another" was revealed regarding two companions of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ who backbit their friend Salman. They had sent him to the Prophet ﷺ to bring them some food. The Prophet ﷺ sent Salman to Usama ibn Zayd, who was in charge of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal). Usama said:

"I have nothing right now." The two men backbit Usama, saying he was stingy, and said regarding Salman: "If we sent him to the well of Samihah (a well abundant with water), its water would recede!" Then they set out themselves to see Usama and spy on the matter. The Prophet ﷺ said, "I see the traces of eating meat in your mouths." They replied, "O Messenger of Allah, we have not eaten any meat today at all!" He said, "Yes, you were eating the flesh of Salman and Usama." Then the verse was revealed, forbidding Muslims from backbiting. [1]

Ridicule, Suspicion, Backbiting, Spying, and Offensive Nicknames are Forbidden!

Since the Holy Quran in this Surah focuses on building an Islamic society based on moral standards, after discussing the duties of Muslims regarding conflicts and disputes between different Islamic groups, the verses under discussion explain some of the roots of these differences so that by severing them, disputes may be eliminated and conflict may end.

In each of the two verses above, three matters that can serve as sparks to ignite the fire of war and discord are addressed with explicit and eloquent expressions.

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 135. Al-Qurtubi also cited this occasion of revelation in his commentary with slight variations.

First, He says: "O you who have believed, let not a people ridicule [another] people" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا يَسْخَرْ قَوْمٌ مِنْ قَوْمٍ).

Because "perhaps they may be better than them" (عَسَىٰ أَنْ يَكُونُوا خَيْرًا مِنْهُمْ).

"Nor let women ridicule [other] women; perhaps they may be better than them" (وَلَا نِسَاءٌ مِنْ نِسَاءٍ عَسَىٰ أَنْ يَكُنَّ خَيْرًا مِنْهُنَّ).

Here, the addressees are the believers, both men and women. The Quran warns everyone to avoid this ugly act, for the source of ridicule and mockery is often a sense of self-superiority, arrogance, and pride, which has been the cause of many bloody wars throughout history.

This "self-superiority" often stems from outward and material values; for instance, one considers himself wealthier, more beautiful, or from a more prominent tribe than another. Occasionally, the delusion of being superior in knowledge, worship, or spirituality drives one to ridicule others, whereas the criterion of value in the sight of Allah is piety (Taqwa), which depends on the purity of the heart, intention, humility, ethics, and manners.

No one can say, "I am superior to so-and-so in the sight of Allah," and for this reason, belittling others and considering oneself superior is one of the worst deeds and ugliest moral defects, the reflections of which may appear in all aspects of human life.

Then, in the second stage, He says: "And do not insult one another" (وَلَا تَلْمِزُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ).

"La talmizu" is derived from the root lamz, meaning to find fault and to taunt. Some have distinguished between hamz and lamz by saying that lamz is counting the faults of individuals in their presence, while hamz is mentioning faults in their absence. It is also said that lamz is fault-finding with the eyes and gestures, while hamz is fault-finding with the tongue (further explanation on this topic will come, God willing, in the commentary of Surah al-Humazah).

Interestingly, the Quran uses the expression "anfusakum" (yourselves) in this verse to point to the unity and integrity of the believers, declaring that all believers are as a single soul; if you find fault with another, you have in reality found fault with yourself!

Finally, in the third stage, He adds: "and do not call each other by [offensive] nicknames" (وَلَا تَنَابَزُوا بِالْأَلْقَابِ).

Many unrestrained individuals, past and present, have insisted on giving others ugly nicknames to belittle them, crush their personality, or perhaps take revenge on them. Or, if someone had a bad past but then repented and became completely pure, they still label him with a nickname that reflects his former state.

Islam explicitly forbids this ugly act and prohibits any name or title that carries the slightest undesirable connotation or serves to belittle a Muslim.

It is mentioned in a Hadith that one day Safiyyah, the daughter of Huyayy ibn Akhtab (the Jewish woman who became a Muslim after the conquest of Khaybar and married the Prophet of Islam ﷺ), came to the Prophet ﷺ weeping.
