The Prophet ﷺ asked her what was wrong. She said, "Aisha reproaches me and says: 'O daughter of a Jew!'" The Prophet ﷺ said, "Why did you not say: 'My father is Aaron, my uncle is

Moses, and my husband is Muhammad ﷺ'?" It was then that this verse was revealed. [1] For this reason, at the end of the verse, He adds: "Wretched is the name of disobedience after [one has reached] faith" (بِئْسَ الِاسْمُ الْفُسُوقُ بَعْدَ الْإِيمَانِ).

Some have suggested another possibility in interpreting this phrase: that Allah forbids believers from accepting the name of "wickedness" (fisq) for themselves due to people's fault-finding after they have believed.

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate given the beginning of the verse and the mentioned occasion of revelation.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis, He says: "And whoever does not repent - then it is those who are the wrongdoers" (وَمَنْ لَمْ يَتُبْ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ).

What injustice is worse than a person hurting the hearts of the faithful—which are the centers of Allah's love—with stinging words, belittlement, and fault-finding, and destroying their character and reputation, which is their greatest asset in life?

We mentioned that in each of the two verses under discussion, three Islamic rulings regarding social ethics are presented. The three rulings of the first verse were: avoiding ridicule, ceasing fault-finding, and avoiding offensive nicknames. The three rulings of the second verse are: avoiding evil suspicion, spying, and backbiting.

In this verse, He first says: "O you who have believed, avoid much [negative] assumption, for indeed, some assumption is sin" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اجْتَنِبُوا كَثِيرًا مِنَ الظَّنِّ إِنَّ بَعْضَ الظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ).

The meaning of "much assumption" refers to evil suspicions, which are more prevalent among people than

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 136.

good assumptions; hence it is expressed as "much." Otherwise, "good assumption" (husn al-zann) is not only not forbidden but is commendable, as the Holy Quran says in Surah al-Nur, verse 12: "Why, when you heard it, did not the believing men and believing women think good of one another?"

It is noteworthy that the prohibition is against "much" assumption, but in the reasoning, He says because "some" assumption is sin. This difference in expression may be because some evil suspicions are consistent with reality and some are contrary to reality. That which is contrary to reality is certainly a sin, and thus it is expressed as "indeed, some assumption is sin." Therefore, the existence of this sin is sufficient reason to avoid all of them.

Here, a question arises: evil or good suspicion is often not voluntary; that is, it reflects in the mind due to a series of premises beyond human control. Therefore, how can it be forbidden?

  1. The purpose of this prohibition is the prohibition of acting upon its effects. That is, whenever an evil suspicion regarding a Muslim arises in your mind, do not give it the slightest regard in practice, do not change your behavior, and do not alter your relations with the person. Therefore, what is a sin is acting upon the evil suspicion.

Thus, we read in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam ﷺ: "There are three things that are not liked in a believer, but there is a way out of them. The way out of evil suspicion is not to realize it (not to act upon it)." [1]

[1]: Al-Mahajjat al-Bayda', Vol. 5, p. 269.

  1. A person can, by reflecting on various matters, distance evil suspicion from himself in many cases. This is done by thinking of ways to interpret actions positively and visualizing valid possibilities for that action in one's mind, gradually overcoming the evil suspicion. Therefore, evil suspicion is not something that is always outside of human control.

Hence, in the narrations, it is commanded to interpret the actions of your brother in the best possible way until evidence to the contrary is established, and never to think ill of a word that comes from your Muslim brother as long as you can find a good interpretation for it. Amir al-Mu'minin عليه السلام said: "Place the affair of your brother upon its best [interpretation] until there comes to you that which overcomes it. And do not think ill of a word that comes from your brother while you can find a good path for it." [1]

In any case, this Islamic instruction is one of the most comprehensive and calculated commands regarding human social relations, fully guaranteeing the issue of security in society, the details of which will come in the discussion of "Key Points."

Then, in the next command, He raises the issue of "prohibition of spying," saying: "And do not spy" (وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا).

Tajassus and tahassus both mean searching, but the former usually applies to undesirable matters, and the latter often to good matters, as Jacob commanded his sons: "O my sons, go and find out (tahassasu) about Joseph and his brother" (Yusuf: 87).

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Accusation and Evil Suspicion, Hadith 3. A similar meaning appears in Nahj al-Balagha with slight differences (Short Sayings, No. 360).

In reality, evil suspicion is a catalyst for searching, and searching is a catalyst for uncovering people's secrets and hidden mysteries, and Islam never allows their private secrets to be exposed.

In other words, Islam wants people to be secure in their private lives from every perspective. Obviously, if everyone were allowed to search into others' affairs, people's dignity and reputation would be destroyed, and a hell would be created in which all members of society would suffer.

Of course, this command does not contradict the existence of intelligence agencies in an Islamic government to combat conspiracies, but this does not mean these agencies have the right to search into people's private lives, as will be explained, God willing.

Finally, in the third and last command, which is in fact the effect and result of the two previous programs, He says: "And do not backbite one another" (وَلَا يَغْتَبْ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضًا).

Thus, evil suspicion is the source of spying, and spying leads to the disclosure of faults and hidden secrets, and awareness of these matters leads to backbiting—all of which Islam has forbidden, both the cause and the effect.

Then, to fully visualize the vileness and ugliness of this act, He places it within an eloquent example, saying: "Would one of you like to eat the flesh of his dead brother?" (أَيُحِبُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَنْ يَأْكُلَ لَحْمَ أَخِيهِ مَيْتًا).

"You would detest it" (فَكَرِهْتُمُوهُ).

Yes, the reputation of a Muslim brother is like the flesh of his body, and destroying this reputation through backbiting and disclosing hidden secrets is like eating his flesh.

The use of the term "dead" is because "backbiting" occurs in the absence of individuals, who, like the dead, are unable to defend themselves.

And this is the most cowardly injustice a person can commit against his brother.

Indeed, this comparison expresses the extraordinary ugliness of backbiting and its immense sin.

In Islamic narrations—as will be mentioned—extraordinary importance is also given to the issue of "backbiting," and there are few sins whose punishment in the view of Islam is as heavy as this.

Since some individuals might be tainted by these three sins and might be awakened by hearing these verses and seek to make amends, He opens the way for them at the end of the verse, saying: "And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Accepting of repentance and Merciful" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ رَحِيمٌ).

First, the spirit of piety and fear of God must be revived, followed by repentance from the sin, so that divine grace and mercy may encompass them.

Key Points

1. Complete and Comprehensive Social Security

The six commands presented in the two verses above (prohibition of ridicule, fault-finding, offensive nicknames, evil suspicion, spying, and backbiting), if fully implemented in a society, insure the reputation and dignity of its members from every angle. No one can use others as a means of amusement and ridicule based on self-superiority, nor can they open their tongue to find fault with this person or that, nor can they use offensive nicknames

to break the sanctity and personality of individuals.

One has no right even to harbor evil suspicion, nor to search into the private lives of individuals, nor to disclose their hidden faults to others.

In other words, a human being possesses four assets that must all be placed within the fortresses of this law and be protected: life, property, honor, and reputation.

The expressions in the above verses and Islamic narrations show that the reputation and dignity of individuals are like their property and lives—rather, in some respects, even more important! Islam wants complete security to prevail in Islamic society; not only should people not attack one another in action and with their hands, but they should also be safe from people's tongues, and even higher, from their thoughts and minds. Everyone should feel that others are not even aiming the arrows of accusation at them in the realm of their thoughts. This is security at the highest level, which is not possible except in a religious and believing society.

The Noble Prophet ﷺ says in a Hadith: "Indeed, Allah has made forbidden for a Muslim his blood, his property, his honor, and that one should harbor evil suspicion about him." [1] Evil suspicion not only harms the other party and his dignity but is also a great calamity for the one who harbors it, as it causes him to be excluded from cooperation with people and social mutual aid, creating a terrifying world filled with alienation and isolation. As it is mentioned in a Hadith from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام: "Whoever does not have good suspicion

[1]: Al-Mahajjat al-Bayda', Vol. 5, p. 268.

is terrified of everyone!" [1] In other words: that which distinguishes human life from animals and gives it vibrancy, movement, and perfection is the spirit of cooperation and collective effort, and this is only possible if trust and optimism prevail among the people, whereas evil suspicion destroys the foundations of this trust, severs the bonds of cooperation, and weakens the social spirit.

Not only evil suspicion, but the issues of spying and backbiting are also like this.

Pessimistic individuals fear everything and are terrified of everyone, and a soul-crushing anxiety constantly dominates their spirits. They can neither find a sympathetic friend and companion, nor a partner and colleague for social activities, nor a helper for a day of helplessness.

It is also necessary to note that the meaning of "suspicion" (zann) here is baseless assumption; therefore, in cases where suspicion is based on evidence—that is, valid suspicion—it is excluded from this ruling, such as the suspicion resulting from the testimony of two just witnesses.

2. Do Not Spy!

We saw that the Quran explicitly forbids spying in the above verse, and since it did not place any conditions or qualifications on it, it shows that searching into the affairs of others and striving to uncover their secrets is a sin. However, the context within and outside the verse shows that this ruling pertains to the personal and private lives of individuals, and it also holds true in social life as long as it does not affect the fate of society.

[1]: Ghurar al-Hikam, p. 697.

But it is clear that where it relates to the fate of others and the integrity of society, the matter takes on a different form. Therefore, the Prophet ﷺ himself appointed agents to gather information, referred to as "eyes" ('uyun), to collect for him whatever related to the fate of the Islamic society internally and externally.

For the same reason, an Islamic government can have intelligence agents or establish an extensive organization for gathering information, and where there is fear of conspiracy against society or endangering the security and Islamic government, they may engage in investigation and even search within the private lives of individuals.

However, this must never become an excuse for breaking the sanctity of this fundamental Islamic law, where individuals, under the pretext of "conspiracy" and "disruption of security," allow themselves to raid people's private lives, open their letters, monitor telephones, and storm their homes at any time.

In short, the boundary between "spying" and "obtaining necessary information to protect the security of society" is very precise and delicate, and those responsible for managing social affairs must strictly observe this boundary so that the sanctity of human secrets is preserved while the security of society and the Islamic government is not endangered.

3. Backbiting is Among the Greatest Sins

We said that man's great asset in life is his dignity, reputation, and personality, and anything that endangers it is as if his life has been endangered; rather, sometimes character assassination is considered more significant than physical assassination, and this

is where its sin is sometimes heavier than murder.

One of the philosophies behind the prohibition of backbiting is so that this great asset is not wasted, the sanctity of persons is not broken, and their dignity is not tarnished—a matter that Islam regards with great importance.

Another point is that "backbiting" creates "pessimism," weakens social bonds, destroys the asset of trust, and destabilizes the foundations of cooperation and mutual aid.

We know that Islam places extraordinary importance on the issue of unity and integrity of the Islamic society and its cohesion and strength. Anything that strengthens this unity is favored by Islam, and anything that weakens it is detested, and backbiting is one of the major factors of weakening.

Beyond these, "backbiting" sows the seeds of hatred and enmity in hearts and sometimes becomes the source of bloody disputes, murder, and killing.

In short, if backbiting is counted in Islam as one of the greatest major sins, it is because of its evil individual and social effects.

In Islamic narrations, very shocking expressions are seen in this regard, a sample of which we provide below:

The Noble Prophet of Islam said: "A dirham that a man earns from usury is greater in sin in the sight of Allah than thirty-six acts of adultery committed by a man! And the highest form of usury is [attacking] the honor of a Muslim man!" [1]

[1]: Al-Mahajjat al-Bayda', Vol. 5, p. 253.

This comparison is because "adultery," however vile and ugly it is, pertains to the "Right of Allah" (Haqq Allah), but usury, and worse than that, destroying people's reputation through backbiting or otherwise, pertains to the "Right of People" (Haqq al-Nas).

In another Hadith, it is mentioned: One day the Prophet ﷺ delivered a sermon with a loud voice and cried out: "O assembly of those who have believed with their tongues but faith has not entered their hearts! Do not backbite Muslims, and do not seek out their private faults, for indeed, whoever seeks out the private faults of his brother, Allah will seek out his private faults, and whomever Allah seeks out his private faults, He will disgrace him in the heart of his own home!" [1]

In another Hadith, it is mentioned that Allah sent a revelation to Moses: "Whoever dies having repented from backbiting will be the last to enter Paradise, and whoever dies persisting in it will be the first to enter the Fire!" [2]

We also read in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam ﷺ: "Backbiting is faster in [destroying] the religion of a Muslim man than gangrene in his vitals!" [3]

This comparison shows that backbiting, like gangrene that eats away and decomposes the flesh of the body,

[1] and [2]: Previous source, p. 252.

[3]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Backbiting, Hadith 1 (Akilah is a type of disease that eats the flesh of the body).

rapidly destroys a person's faith. Considering that the motives for backbiting are things like envy, arrogance, stinginess, malice, monopolization, and similar ugly and blameworthy traits, it becomes clear why backbiting and destroying the reputation and respect of Muslims in this way so thoroughly destroys a person's faith (reflect on this).

Narrations in this regard in Islamic sources are very numerous; we conclude this discussion by mentioning one more Hadith. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "Whoever relates a story about a believer intending thereby to shame him and destroy his manliness so that he falls from the eyes of the people, Allah will expel him from His guardianship to the guardianship of Satan, and even Satan will not accept him!" [1]

All these emphases and shocking phrases are due to the extraordinary importance Islam places on preserving the reputation and social dignity of believers, and also because of the destructive effect backbiting has on the unity of society, mutual trust, and the bonding of hearts. Worse still, backbiting is a factor in fanning the flames of malice, enmity, hostility, hypocrisy, and the spread of indecency at the social level, because when people's hidden faults are revealed through backbiting, the gravity and magnitude of the sin diminish, and becoming tainted by it becomes easy.

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 8, Chapter 157, Hadith 2, p. 608.

4. The Concept of Backbiting

"Backbiting" (Ghibah), as its name suggests, is to speak of someone in their absence, specifically speaking of a fault among their faults, whether this fault is physical, moral, in their actions, or in their speech, and even in matters related to them such as clothing, house, spouse, children, and the like.

Therefore, if someone describes the apparent and manifest traits of another, it will not be backbiting, unless the intention is to disparage and find fault, in which case it is forbidden—such as saying disparagingly: "that blind man," or "short man," or "black-skinned," or "scant-bearded." Thus, mentioning hidden faults with whatever intention is backbiting and forbidden, and mentioning manifest faults with the intention of disparagement is also forbidden, whether we include it in the definition of backbiting or not.

All of this applies if these traits are actually present in the person; however, if a trait does not exist at all, it falls under the category of "slander" (Tuhmah), the sin of which is far more severe and heavy.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Backbiting is to say about your brother what Allah has concealed for him; but as for an apparent matter in him, such as sharp-temperedness or haste, then no. And slander (Buhtan) is to say what is not in him." [1]

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Backbiting and Slander, Hadith 7.

From this, it becomes clear that the common excuses some people bring for backbiting are not acceptable. For example, sometimes the backbiter says, "This is not backbiting, but rather his trait!" whereas if it were not his trait, it would be slander, not backbiting.

Or he says: "This is something I would say in his presence as well," whereas saying it to the person's face not only does not reduce the sin of backbiting but, due to the causing of hurt (idha'), brings about an even heavier sin.

5. The Remedy for Backbiting and Repentance from It

"Backbiting," like many blameworthy traits, gradually becomes a psychological illness, such that the backbiter takes pleasure in his work and feels satisfaction and joy in constantly destroying the reputation of this person or that. This is one of the very dangerous moral stages.

At this point, the backbiter must, before anything else, treat the internal motives of backbiting that are in the depths of his soul and fuel this sin—motives such as "stinginess," "envy," "malice," "enmity," and "self-superiority."

He must, through self-purification and reflecting on the evil consequences of these ugly traits and the disastrous results they produce, as well as through spiritual discipline, wash these impurities from his soul and heart so that he can restrain his tongue from being tainted by backbiting.

Then he should seek repentance. Since backbiting involves the "Right of People," if he has access to the victim of the backbiting and it does not create a new problem, he should apologize to him, even if in general terms—for example, by saying, "I have sometimes backbit you out of ignorance

and lack of awareness; please forgive me"—and he should not provide more detail, lest it become a cause for new corruption.

If he does not have access to the person, or does not know him, or the person has passed away, he should seek forgiveness for him and perform good deeds, perhaps by the blessing of which Allah the Almighty will forgive him and make the other party satisfied.

6. Cases of Exception

The final word on backbiting is that the law of backbiting, like any other law, has exceptions. Among them is when one is in the position of "consultation"—for example, for choosing a spouse, or a partner in business and the like—and someone asks a question. Trustworthiness in consultation, which is an established Islamic law, requires that if one knows of faults in the party, he should mention them, lest a Muslim fall into a trap; such backbiting performed with such an intention is not forbidden.

Likewise, in other cases where important goals like the goal of consultation are at stake, or for the sake of claiming a right or seeking redress for an injustice.

Of course, one who openly commits sins and is what is termed "notoriously sinful" (mutajahir bi al-fisq) is outside the subject of backbiting, and if his sin is mentioned behind his back, there is no objection. However, it must be noted that this ruling is specific to the sin he commits openly.

It is also noteworthy that not only is backbiting forbidden, but listening to backbiting and being present in a gathering of backbiting is also among the prohibitions. Rather,

according to some narrations, it is obligatory for Muslims to reject the backbiting—that is, to rise in defense against the backbiting and defend the Muslim brother whose reputation is endangered. How beautiful is a society in which these moral principles are strictly implemented.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ وَأُنثَىٰ وَجَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَآئِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ أَتْقَىٰكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌ (١٣)
O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most Godwary among you. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-aware. (13)

Commentary

Piety, the Greatest Human Value

In the preceding verses, the discourse was addressed to the believers with the call, "O you who have attained faith" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا), and it recounted and forbade several actions that threaten a "believing society."

In the verse under discussion, however, the addressee is the entire human society. It expresses the most important principle that guarantees order and stability, and it defines the true criterion of human values against false and deceptive ones.

It says: "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you peoples and tribes that you may know one another" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثَىٰ وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَائِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوا).

The meaning of creating people from a male and a female refers to the lineage of all humans tracing back to "Adam" and "Eve." Therefore, since everyone originates from a single root, it makes no sense to boast over one another regarding lineage or tribe. If Allah has created specific characteristics for every tribe and group, it is for the sake of preserving the order of social life, because these differences are a means of identification. Without the identification of individuals, order would not govern human society; for if everyone were identical and looked exactly alike, a massive chaos would engulf the entire human community.

Regarding the difference between Shu‘ub (plural of Sha‘b), meaning "large groups of people," and Qaba’il (plural of Qabilah), meaning "tribes," commentators have offered various possibilities.

Some have said the scope of Shu‘ub is broader than Qaba’il, just as the word Sha‘b is applied today to a "nation."

Others consider Shu‘ub to refer to "non-Arab groups" and Qaba’il to refer to "Arab groups."

Finally, some others have regarded Shu‘ub in terms of a person's affiliation with geographical regions, and Qaba’il as referring to affiliation with race and blood.

However, the first interpretation seems the most appropriate of all.

In any case, after the Holy Quran nullifies the greatest source of pride and boasting of the Age of Ignorance—namely lineage and tribe—it turns to the real criterion of value and adds: "Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you" (إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ أَتْقَاكُمْ).

In this way, it draws a red line through all superficial and material distinctions, giving authenticity and reality to the issue of piety (Taqwa), righteousness, and the fear of God. It declares that for attaining proximity to Allah and nearness to His sacred presence, no distinction is effective except piety.

And since piety is a spiritual and internal quality that must first be established in the heart and soul of a human, and because there may be many claimants but few who truly possess it, the end of the verse adds: "Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Aware" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌ).

He knows the pious well, and He is aware of the degree of their piety, sincerity of intention, purity, and clarity. He honors and rewards them according to His knowledge, just as He knows the false claimants and punishes them.

Key Points

1- True Values and False Values

Undoubtedly, every human being instinctively desires to be a valuable and honorable being, and for this reason, they strive with all their might to acquire values.

However, the recognition of the criterion of value differs completely with the variation of cultures, and sometimes false values take the place of true ones.

One group considers their real value to lie in affiliation with a "famous and prestigious tribe," and thus they constantly struggle to glorify the status of their tribe and clan, so that by magnifying it, they may magnify themselves through that affiliation.

Particularly among the people of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), boasting about lineages and tribes was the most common illusory pride, to the extent that every tribe considered itself the "superior tribe" and every race considered itself the "higher race." Unfortunately, the sediments and remnants of this still exist in the depths of the souls of many individuals and nations.

Another group considers the matter of wealth, property, possessing palaces, castles, servants, and similar things as signs of value, and they constantly strive for them. Meanwhile, another group regards high social and political positions as the criterion of personality.

In this manner, every group walks a certain path, sets their heart on a value, and considers it the criterion.

But since these matters are all unstable, extrinsic, material, and fleeting, a heavenly religion like Islam can never agree with them. Therefore, it draws a line of cancellation over all of them and considers the real value of a human to be in their intrinsic qualities, especially piety, righteousness, commitment, and purity. Even for important subjects like science and knowledge, if they are not placed in the path of faith, piety, and moral values, it does not grant them importance.

It is remarkable that the Quran appeared in an environment where the value of the "tribe" was considered the most important of all values, yet it shattered this fabricated idol and freed man from the captivity of "blood," "tribe," "color," "race," "wealth," "position," and "riches," leading him into his own soul and his lofty qualities to find himself!

Interestingly, in the circumstances of revelation (Sha’n al-Nuzul) mentioned for this verse, points are seen that reflect the depth of this Islamic instruction. Among them: after the conquest of Mecca, the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the call to prayer (Adhan) to be given. Bilal went onto the roof of the Kaaba and gave the call. Attab bin Asid, who was among the freed captives, said: "I thank God that my father passed away and did not see such a day!" And Harith bin Hisham said: "Could the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله find no one other than this 'black crow'?" (The above verse was revealed and expressed the criterion of true value) [1].

Others have said: The verse was revealed when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered a girl to be given in marriage to one of the Mawali (freed slaves or non-Arabs). They were surprised and said: "O Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, do you command us to give our daughters to the Mawali?" (The verse was revealed and drew a line of cancellation over these superstitious thoughts) [1].

In a Hadith, we read: One day the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله delivered a sermon to the people in Mecca and said: "O people! Allah has removed from you the shame of the Age of Ignorance and its boasting about fathers. People are but two types: a righteous, pious man who is noble in the sight of Allah, or a wicked, wretched man who is insignificant in the sight of the Truth. All people are children of Adam, and Allah created Adam from dust, as Allah Almighty says: 'O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female, and made you peoples and tribes that you may know one another. Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you. Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Aware.'" [2]

In the book Adab al-Nufus by Tabari, it is mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, during the days of Tashriq (the 11th, 12th, and 13th of Dhu al-Hijjah) in the land of Mina, while mounted on a camel, turned to the people and said: "O people, know! Your Lord is One and your father is one. Indeed, there is no superiority for an Arab over a non-Arab, nor for a non-Arab over an Arab, nor for a black person over a ruddy person, nor for a ruddy person over a black person, except by piety. Have I conveyed the message?" They all said: "Yes!" He said: "Let those present convey this to those who are absent." [1]

And in another Hadith, in short and meaningful sentences from him, it is stated: "Indeed, Allah does not look at your status, nor at your lineages, nor at your bodies, nor at your wealth, but He looks at your hearts. Whoever has a righteous heart, Allah shows him grace. You are all children of Adam, and the most beloved of you to Him is the most pious of you." [2]

Yet it is strange that despite these extensive, rich, and fruitful teachings, there are still those among Muslims who emphasize the issues of "race," "blood," and "language," and even prioritize their unity over Islamic brotherhood and religious unity, reviving the fanaticism of the Age of Ignorance. Despite the heavy blows they have received from this path, it is as if they do not wish to wake up and return to the decree of Islam!

May Allah protect everyone from the evil of the fanaticisms of the Age of Ignorance.

Islam has fought against "Jahiliyyah fanaticism" in every shape and form, in order to gather the Muslims of the world from every race, nation, and tribe under a single banner—not the banner of nationalism or race, nor any other banner—because Islam never accepts these narrow and limited perspectives and considers them all illusory and baseless. It is even mentioned in a Hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said regarding Jahiliyyah fanaticism: "Leave it, for it is a foul-smelling thing!" [1]

But why is this foul-smelling thought still cherished by a large group who outwardly consider themselves Muslims and speak of the Quran and Islamic brotherhood? It is not clear!

How beautiful is a society built on the Islamic value criterion of "Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you," where the false values of race, wealth, riches, geographical regions, and class are abolished. Yes, God-wariness, an internal sense of responsibility, standing against desires, and commitment to truth, integrity, purity, right, and justice—this is the only criterion of human value and nothing else. Although in the chaotic market of current societies, this authentic value has been forgotten, and false values have taken its place.

In the value system of Ignorance, which revolved around "boasting about ancestors, wealth, and children," a handful of thieves and plunderers were nurtured. But with the transformation of this system and the revival of the lofty principle of "Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you," its product was human beings like Salman, Abu Dharr, Ammar Yasir, and Miqdad.

What is important in the revolution of human societies is the revolution of its value system and the revival of this authentic Islamic principle.

We end this discussion with a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله where he said: "All of you are children of Adam, and Adam was created from dust. Let people cease boasting about their forefathers, or they will be more insignificant to Allah than the beetles that roll in filth!" [1]

2- The Reality of Piety (Taqwa)

As we have seen, the Quran has placed the greatest distinction on piety and considers it the only criterion for measuring the value of human beings.

Elsewhere, it considers piety the best provision, saying: "And take provisions, but indeed, the best provision is piety" (Baqarah: 197).

And in another place, it considers the garment of piety the best garment for man: "And the garment of piety—that is best" (A'raf: 26).

In numerous verses, it mentions "piety" as one of the first principles of the prophets' call, and finally, in another place, it raises the importance of this subject to the extent that it considers Allah as the "Source of Piety," saying: "He is the Source of Piety and the Source of Forgiveness" (Muddaththir: 56).

The Quran considers "piety" a divine light that, wherever it becomes rooted, creates knowledge and wisdom: "And fear Allah, and Allah teaches you" (Baqarah: 282).

It also pairs "goodness" and "piety" together: "And cooperate in righteousness and piety."

And it mentions "justice" alongside "piety": "Be just; that is nearer to piety."

Now we must see what the reality of piety is—this great spiritual capital and this greatest human honor with all these distinctions.

The Quran provides indications that unveil the reality of piety: in several verses, it considers the place of piety to be the "heart." Among them, it says: "Those are the ones whose hearts Allah has tested for piety" (Hujurat: 3).

The Quran mentions "piety" as the opposite of "wickedness" (Fujur), as we read in Surah Ash-Shams, verse 8: "Then He inspired it with its wickedness and its piety."

The Quran considers every action that stems from the spirit of sincerity, faith, and pure intention to be based on "piety," as it says in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 108, regarding the Quba Mosque (which the hypocrites built the Dirar Mosque to oppose): "A mosque founded on piety from the first day is more worthy for you to stand in."

From the sum of these verses, it is clearly understood that "piety" is that sense of responsibility and commitment that governs a person's existence following the rooting of faith in the heart. It restrains him from "wickedness" and sin, invites him to goodness, purity, and justice, and purifies his deeds and his thoughts and intentions from pollutions.

When we return to the linguistic root of this word, we reach the same conclusion, for Taqwa comes from Wiqayah, meaning the effort to preserve and protect something. In these contexts, it means protecting the soul and spirit from any kind of pollution and concentrating one's powers on matters in which the pleasure of Allah lies.

Some scholars have identified three stages for piety: 1. Protecting the self from eternal punishment through the acquisition of correct beliefs. 2. Avoiding every kind of sin, whether it be neglecting an obligation or committing a transgression. 3. Self-restraint against whatever occupies the human heart and diverts it from the Truth; this is the piety of the elite, or rather the elite of the elite. [1]

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام (in Nahj al-Balagha), has eloquent and vivid expressions regarding piety, and piety is one of the issues emphasized in many of his sermons, letters, and short sayings.

In one place, he compares piety with sin and pollution, saying: "Behold, indeed sins are like rebellious horses upon which their riders are placed, and their reins are removed, so they plunge them into the Fire! Behold, indeed piety is like docile mounts upon which their riders are placed, and they are given their reins, so they lead them into Paradise!" [2]

According to this subtle metaphor, piety is that state of self-restraint, self-control, and mastery over desires, while lack of piety is surrendering to rebellious desires and the loss of any control over them.

In another place, he says: "Know, O servants of Allah, that piety is a mighty fortress, and wickedness is a weak fortress that does not protect its inhabitants and does not safeguard those who take refuge in it. Behold, it is only through piety that the sting of sins is severed." [1]

And again, in another place, he adds: "So hold fast to the piety of Allah, for it has a strong rope whose handle is firm and a lofty stronghold whose peak is inaccessible." [2]

From the midst of all these expressions, the reality and spirit of piety become clearly evident.

It is also necessary to remind that piety is the fruit of the tree of faith, and for this reason, to obtain this great capital, the foundation of faith must be made firm.

Of course, practice in obedience, avoidance of sin, and attention to moral programs root piety in the soul, and the result is the emergence of the light of certainty and visionary faith in the human spirit. The more the light of "piety" increases, the light of "certainty" will also increase. Therefore, we see in Islamic narrations that "piety" is considered one degree higher than "faith" and one degree lower than "certainty"!

Imam Ali bin Musa al-Rida عليه السلام says: "Faith is one degree above Islam, and piety is one degree above faith, and certainty is one degree above piety, and nothing has been distributed among people less than certainty." [1]

We end this discussion with a famous poem that expresses the reality of piety through a clear example: "Leave sins, both small and great; that is piety." "And be like one walking upon a land of thorns, wary of what he sees." "Never belittle a small sin, for indeed mountains are made of small pebbles!"

Ayahs

۞ قَالَتِ ٱلْأَعْرَابُ ءَامَنَّا ۖ قُل لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَلَـٰكِن قُولُوٓا۟ أَسْلَمْنَا وَلَمَّا يَدْخُلِ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنُ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ لَا يَلِتْكُم مِّنْ أَعْمَـٰلِكُمْ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٤)
The Bedouins say, ‘We have faith.’ Say, ‘You do not have faith yet; rather, say, ‘‘We have embraced Islam,’’ for faith has not yet entered into your hearts. Yet if you obey Allah and His Apostle, He will not stint anything of [the reward of] your works. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (14)
إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَرْتَابُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقُونَ (١٥)
The faithful are only those who have attained faith in Allah and His Apostle and then have never doubted, and who wage jihad with their possessions and their persons in the way of Allah. It is they who are the truthful. (15)

Occasion of Revelation

Many commentators have mentioned an occasion of revelation for this verse, the summary of which is as follows:

A group from the "Bani Asad" tribe entered Medina during a year of famine and drought. Hoping to receive aid from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they uttered the Shahadatayn (the two testimonies of faith). They said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The Arab tribes mounted their steeds and fought against you, but we came to you with our wives and children and did not engage in war." Through this, they intended to place a burden of obligation upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The above verses were revealed (reminding them that their Islam is merely outward and that faith has not reached the depths of their hearts. Furthermore, even if they have believed, they must not place a burden of obligation upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, Allah has a favor upon them for guiding them) [^1]. However, the existence of this occasion of revelation—like other instances—never prevents the generality of the verse's meaning.

Commentary

The Difference Between "Islam" and "Iman" (Faith)

In the preceding verse, the discussion centered on the criterion for human value, namely "Taqwa" (piety/God-wariness). Since "Taqwa" is the fruit of the tree of "Iman" (faith)—specifically a faith that penetrates the depths of the soul—the verses under discussion proceed to explain the reality of "Iman" as follows:

"The desert Arabs say, 'We have believed.' Tell them: 'You have not believed; rather say, "We have submitted (accepted Islam)," for faith has not yet entered your hearts!'" (قالَتِ الْأَعْرابُ آمَنَّا قُلْ لَمْ تُؤْمِنُوا وَ لكِنْ قُولُوا أَسْلَمْنا وَ لَمَّا يَدْخُلِ الْإِيمانُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ).

According to this verse, the difference between "Islam" and "Iman" is that "Islam" has a legal and outward form; anyone who utters the Shahadatayn enters the fold of Muslims, and the rulings of Islam are applied to them.

However, "Iman" is a real and internal matter, and its locus is the human heart, not the tongue or the outward appearance.

"Islam" may stem from various motives, even material motives and personal interests, but "Iman" must certainly spring from spiritual motives, from knowledge and awareness; it is this very faith upon whose branches the life-giving fruit of Taqwa appears.

This is the same point mentioned in an expressive statement from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

الاسلام علانية، و الايمان فى القلب:

"Islam is an outward matter, but the place of faith is the heart." [^1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

الاسلام يحقن به الدم و تؤدى به الامانة، و تستحل به الفروج، و الثواب على الايمان:

"Through Islam, a person's blood is protected, the returning of trusts to them becomes obligatory, and marriage with them becomes lawful; but the reward [in the Hereafter] is based on faith." [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Ruh al-Bayan, and Fi Zilal regarding the verses under discussion. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 138. [^2]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 2, "Chapter: Indeed Islam Protects the Blood," Hadith 1 and 2.

It is for this same reason that in some narrations, the concept of "Islam" is limited to verbal confession, while faith is introduced as confession coupled with action:

(الايمان اقرار و عمل، و الاسلام اقرار بلا عمل)

"Faith is confession and action, and Islam is confession without action." [^1] This same meaning has appeared in another expression regarding the discussion of "Islam and Iman." Fudayl ibn Yasar says: I heard Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام say:

ان الايمان يشارك الاسلام، و لا يشاركه الاسلام، ان الايمان ما وقر فى القلوب، و الاسلام ما عليه المناكح و المواريث و حقن الدماء:

"Faith shares with Islam, but Islam does not share with faith (in other words, every believer is a Muslim, but not every Muslim is a believer). 'Faith' is that which settles in the heart, but 'Islam' is that upon which the laws of marriage, inheritance, and the protection of blood are applied." [^2] However, this conceptual difference applies when these two terms are placed in contrast to one another; but whenever they are mentioned separately, Islam may be applied to the same thing that faith is applied to—meaning both terms are used with one meaning.

Then, in the verse under discussion, it adds: "If you obey Allah and His Messenger, He will give you the reward of your deeds in full, and He will not diminish anything from the reward of your deeds" (وَ إِنْ تُطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ لا يَلِتْكُمْ مِنْ أَعْمالِكُمْ شَيْئاً).

Because "Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

"La yalitkum" is derived from the root "layt" (rhyming with rayb), meaning to diminish a right. [^1] These final phrases are, in fact, an allusion to an established Quranic principle that the condition for the acceptance of deeds is "Iman." It says that if you have heart-felt faith in Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—the sign of which is obedience to the command of Allah and His Messenger—your deeds gain value, and Allah accepts and rewards even your smallest good deeds, and even forgives your sins by the blessing of this faith, for He is Forgiving and Merciful.

And since attaining this internal matter—namely faith—is not an easy task, in the next verse, He proceeds to mention its signs; signs that clearly distinguish the believer (Mu'min) from the submitter (Muslim), the truthful from the liar, and those who have lovingly accepted the call of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from those who profess faith to save their lives or to attain worldly wealth. He says:

"The true believers are only those who have believed in Allah and His Messenger, then have never doubted, and have striven with their wealth and their lives in the way of Allah" (إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَرْتابُوا وَ جاهَدُوا بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ).

Yes, the first sign of faith is the absence of hesitation and doubt on the path of Islam. The second sign is Jihad with wealth, and the third sign—which is the highest of all—is Jihad with one's self (lives).

In this way, Islam has sought out the clearest signs: steadfastness and firmness of step, and the absence of doubt and hesitation on one hand, and the sacrifice of wealth and life on the other.

How is it possible for faith not to be firmly rooted in the heart when a person does not withhold the sacrifice of wealth and life in the way of the Beloved?

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He adds: "Such are the truthful ones," and the spirit of faith surges within their being (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصَّادِقُونَ).

This criterion, which the Quran has expressed for distinguishing "truthful believers" from "liars pretending to be Muslims," is not limited to the poor of the "Bani Asad" tribe. It is a clear and eloquent criterion for every age and time, for separating real believers from false claimants, and for showing the value of the claims of those who everywhere boast of Islam and consider the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله indebted to them, yet in practice, not the slightest sign of faith or Islam is seen in them.

In contrast, there are those who not only make no claims but always consider themselves falling short, yet they are the foremost in the field of sacrifice and devotion.

If we were to apply this Quranic criterion to measure real believers, it is not clear how many out of the vast millions upon millions of claimants to Islam are real believers, and how many are merely outward Muslims!

[^1]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 2, "Chapter: Indeed Islam Protects the Blood," Hadith 1 and 2. [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, "Chapter: Indeed Faith Shares with Islam," Hadith 3. [^1]: Therefore, the said verb is Ajwaf Ya'i, although the root "walat" (Mithal Wawi) has also appeared with the same meaning.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَتُعَلِّمُونَ ٱللَّهَ بِدِينِكُمْ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١٦)
Say, ‘Will you inform Allah about your faith while Allah knows whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth, and Allah has knowledge of all things?’ (16)
يَمُنُّونَ عَلَيْكَ أَنْ أَسْلَمُوا۟ ۖ قُل لَّا تَمُنُّوا۟ عَلَىَّ إِسْلَـٰمَكُم ۖ بَلِ ٱللَّهُ يَمُنُّ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْ هَدَىٰكُمْ لِلْإِيمَـٰنِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٧)
They count it as a favour to you that they have embraced Islam. Say, ‘Do not count your embracing of Islam as a favour to me. No, it is Allah who has done you a favour in that He has guided you to faith, if you are truthful [in your claim]. (17)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٨)
Indeed Allah knows the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and Allah watches what you do.’ (18)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators have stated that after the revelation of the preceding verses, a group of Arabs came to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and swore oaths that they were sincere in their claim of faith, and that their outward appearance matched their inner reality. Consequently, the first verse under discussion was revealed (warning them that there is no need for oaths, as Allah knows the hidden and the manifest of everyone). [^1]

Commentary

Do Not Deem Your Submission a Favor!

In the previous verses, the signs of true believers were explained. As mentioned in the occasion of revelation, a group of claimants insisted that the reality of faith was established in their hearts. The Quran announces to them and to all those like them that there is no need for insistence or oaths. In the matter of "faith" and "disbelief," you are dealing with a God Who is aware of everything. Specifically, with a reproachful tone, the first verse under discussion says: "Say: 'Would you inform Allah of your religion, while Allah knows all that is in the heavens and all that is on the earth?'" (قُلْ أَ تُعَلِّمُونَ اللَّهَ بِدِينِكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

And for further emphasis, It adds: "And Allah is All-Knowing of all things" (وَ اللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ‌).

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 22; p. 215

His Holy Essence is the very essence of knowledge, and His knowledge is the very essence of His Being; for this reason, His knowledge is eternal and everlasting.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Al-Mizan, Ruh al-Bayan, and Tafsir al-Qurtubi.

His Pure Essence is present everywhere; He is closer to you than your jugular vein and intervenes between a man and his heart. Thus, there is no need for your claims. He knows the truthful from the false claimants perfectly well and is aware of the depths of their souls. Even the degrees of intensity or weakness of their faith, which are sometimes hidden from themselves, are clear to Him. Despite this, why do you insist on informing Allah of your faith?!

Then, the discourse returns to the Bedouin Arabs who were flaunting their Islam to the Prophet, saying: "We came to you in submission while many Arab tribes came to you in war." The Quran says in response to them: "They deem it a favor to you that they have embraced Islam!" (يَمُنُّونَ عَلَيْكَ أَنْ أَسْلَمُوا).

"Say: 'Do not deem your Islam a favor to me'" (قُلْ لا تَمُنُّوا عَلَيَّ إِسْلامَكُمْ‌).

"Rather, Allah confers a favor upon you that He has guided you to faith, if you are truthful [in your claim of faith]!" (بَلِ اللَّهُ يَمُنُّ عليكم أَنْ هَداكُمْ لِلْإِيمانِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌).

"Favor" (Mann)—as we have mentioned before—is derived from the root mann, meaning a specific weight used for measuring. Subsequently, it was applied to any heavy and precious blessing. Favor is of two kinds: if it is practical (meaning the granting of a precious blessing), it is praiseworthy, and Divine favors are of this type. However, if it is verbal, as is the case with many humans, it is a foul and disliked act.

It is interesting that in the first sentence, It says they deem it a favor to you that they have accepted "Islam," which is another emphasis that they are not sincere in their claim of faith, but have only outwardly accepted Islam.

However, at the end of the verse, It says: "If you are truthful in your claim, Allah confers a favor upon you that He has guided you to 'faith'."

In any case, this is an important issue: short-sighted individuals often imagine that by accepting faith and performing acts of worship and obedience, they have rendered a service to the Divine Presence or to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and his successors عليه السلام, and for this reason, they expect a reward.

In reality, if the light of faith shines upon someone's heart and they are granted the success to join the ranks of the believers, the greatest Divine grace has encompassed them.

"Faith," before anything else, gives man a new perception of the universe; it sets aside the veils and curtains of selfishness and pride, opens man's horizon of vision, and visualizes the peerless glory and grandeur of creation before him.

Then, it sprinkles light and brightness upon his emotions and nurtures them, revives human values within him, blossoms his lofty talents, and grants him knowledge, power, courage, altruism, sacrifice, forgiveness, and sincerity, transforming a weak being into a powerful and fruitful human.

It takes his hand and elevates him through the stages of perfection, reaching the peak of the summit of honor. It makes him harmonious with the laws of the universe and places the universe under his command.

Is this a blessing that Allah has bestowed upon man, or a favor that man confers upon the Prophet of Allah صلي الله عليه وآله?!

Likewise, every act of worship and obedience is a step toward perfection: it purifies the heart, controls desires, strengthens the spirit of sincerity, and grants unity, integrity, strength, and greatness to the Islamic society.

Each one is a great educational class and an instructive lesson.

It is here that man must give thanks for the blessing of faith every morning and evening, and after every prayer and every act of worship, he should place his head in prostration and thank Allah for all this success.

If man's insight regarding faith and obedience to Allah is such, he will not only not consider himself a "creditor," but will always consider himself "indebted" to Allah and the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and immersed in His kindness.

He performs acts of worship passionately, and in the path of His obedience, he runs not with his feet, but with his head. And if Allah has designated a reward for his actions, he considers this yet another grace; otherwise, the benefit of performing good deeds returns to man himself, and in truth, with this success, the amount of his debts to Allah increases.

Therefore, His guidance is a grace, the invitation of His Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله is another grace, the success of obedience and compliance is a double grace, and the reward is a grace beyond grace!

In the final verse under discussion, which is the end of Surah al-Hujurat, It again emphasizes what was mentioned in the previous verse, saying: "Indeed, Allah knows the unseen of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is All-Seeing of what you do" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ‌). Do not insist that you are definitely believers, and there is no need for oaths.

He is present in the corners of your heart and is fully aware of what passes within it. He is aware of all the secrets of the depths of the earth and the unseen of the heavens; therefore, how is it possible for Him to be unaware of the inside of your heart?

O Allah! You have conferred a favor upon us and shone the light of faith into our hearts. We beseech You by the great blessing of guidance to keep us firm on this path and lead us in the course of perfection! O Lord! You are aware of the depths of our hearts and know our intentions well; conceal our faults from Your servants and rectify them by Your generosity! O God! Grant us the success and power to revive within ourselves the great moral values You have expressed in this magnificent Surah and to safeguard its sanctity. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Hujurat, 1st of Muharram, year 1406.

(50) Surah Qaf: This Surah was revealed in "Makkah" and contains 45 verses.

Content of Surah Qaf

The axis of the discussions in this Surah is the issue of "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), and almost all the verses revolve around this axis, while other matters take a secondary form.

Regarding issues related to the Resurrection, it highlights the following matters:

  1. The denial and astonishment of the disbelievers regarding the issue of Resurrection (the physical Resurrection).

  2. Argumentation for the issue of Resurrection through attention to the system of creation, especially the revival of dead lands by the falling of rain.

  3. Argumentation for the issue of Resurrection through attention to the initial creation.

  4. Reference to the recording of deeds and words for the "Day of Reckoning."

  5. Matters related to death and the transition from this world to the other abode.

  6. A glimpse into the events of the Day of Judgment and the descriptions of Paradise and Hell.

  7. Reference to the soul-shaking events of the end of the world, which serve as a beginning for the other world.

In the midst of this, there are brief and effective references to the state of previous rebellious nations and their painful and ominous fate (such as the people of Pharaoh, 'Ad, Lut, Shu'ayb, and Tubba'), as well as instructions given to the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله to pay attention to Allah and His remembrance.

A brief reference to the greatness of the Quran is also seen at the beginning and end of the Surah.

Virtue of Reciting Surah Qaf

It is understood from Islamic narrations that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله attached great importance to this Surah, to the extent that he would recite it every Friday during the Friday prayer sermon. [^1] In another tradition, it is mentioned that he would recite it on every "Eid" and "Friday." [^2] This is because Friday and Eid are days of awakening and awareness for humans, days of returning to the original nature (fitrah), and days of attention to Allah and the Day of Reckoning. Since the verses of this Surah recount the issue of Resurrection, death, and the events of Judgment Day in a very effective manner, and reflecting upon them has a deep impact on the awakening and education of humans, it was under his special attention.

In a tradition from the Holy Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, it is narrated:

"Whoever recites Surah Qaf, Allah will ease for him the throes and agonies of death." [^3]

Also, in a tradition from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is stated:

"Whoever constantly recites Surah Qaf in his obligatory and supererogatory prayers, Allah will expand his sustenance, give him his record of deeds in his right hand,

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6171.

[^2]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 7, p. 547.

[^3]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 140.

and settle his account on the Day of Resurrection with an easy reckoning." [^1]

It goes without saying that all this honor and virtue is not achieved merely by reciting the words; rather, reciting the words is a beginning for the awakening of thoughts, which in turn is a means for righteous action and harmony with the content of the Surah.
